1	# Experimental infrastructure: setup, artifacts, lifecycle
2	
3	**Status:** draft (2026-04-30) — refined with Sean's feedback.
4	
5	## Purpose
6	
7	This document specifies the infrastructure for running framework-side
8	experiments — runs that need a controlled codex, controlled framework
9	state, isolated session recording, and reproducible setup. The immediate
10	driver is P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`), whose
11	sub-questions (orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity,
12	cost confirmation) all require executing real implementer sessions in
13	isolated conditions before the architecture can be designed empirically.
14	Subsequent experiments touching framework or commission-pipeline behavior
15	will reuse the same infrastructure.
16	
17	The infrastructure does **not** govern paper analyses (instruments run
18	against existing transcript archives) — those need no setup. It governs
19	work that **executes new commissions** under controlled conditions.
20	
21	## Vocabulary
22	
23	- **Experiment.** One piece of inquiry — a research question plus the
24	  analytical frame to answer it. Lives at
25	  `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/`.
26	- **Run.** One execution of the experimental apparatus with a specific
27	  set of variable settings. An experiment produces one or more runs.
28	  Lives at `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/`.
29	- **Variables.** The inputs that define a run. Split into **controlled**
30	  (held constant — framework SHA, codex base, common config) and
31	  **manipulated** (varied per run — prompts, plugin versions, config
32	  fragments). Both kinds live in `run.yaml`.
33	
34	## Design principles
35	
36	1. **Three-repo separation.** The sanctum, the framework (nexus), and the
37	   codex live in three repos with distinct persistence rules. Each artifact
38	   has one canonical home.
39	2. **One guild per run.** Every run gets its own fresh guild and its
40	   own short-lived codex repo. This eliminates run-isolation problems
41	   inside the guild's books — every row in the guild DB belongs to this
42	   run by construction.
43	3. **Sanctum is the canonical record.** Methodology, manifests, input
44	   files, captured execution data, and analysis live in the sanctum. The
45	   other two repos (nexus branches, codex repos) are reproducibility
46	   breadcrumbs.
47	4. **Reproducibility via SHA pinning.** Every framework dep, every codex
48	   base, every plugin version is pinned to a SHA in a per-run manifest
49	   that lives in the sanctum.
50	5. **Disposable execution surfaces.** Codex repos and live guild dirs are
51	   short-lived. They're created at run start, archived to sanctum at
52	   run end, and deleted. Re-running an experiment doesn't depend on
53	   long-lived disposable state.
54	6. **Standard guild mechanisms over bespoke generation.** The setup
55	   script composes existing `nsg` commands (`nsg init`, `nsg plugin
56	   install`, `nsg codex add`) and Laboratory tools rather than
57	   hand-generating `guild.json` / `package.json`. Custom guild
58	   configuration is expressed as a partial `guild.json` merge plus a
59	   list of files to copy.
60	
61	## Architecture
62	
63	### The three repos
64	
65	| repo | role | persistence |
66	|---|---|---|
67	| **Sanctum** (`nexus-mk2`) | methodology, manifests, input files, captured artifacts, analysis, findings | indefinite; canonical research record |
68	| **Nexus framework** | the framework state that produces experimental variance. Each run declares whatever branch + base SHA it needs. Runs that share framework state share a branch; runs that introduce framework variance get their own. No enforced cardinality. | indefinite; archived branches |
69	| **Codex repo** | short-lived fresh repo (clone of the upstream codex, pushed as a new repo — *not* a GitHub fork, to avoid leaving fork metadata on the upstream). One per run. The repo's `main` branch is the run's working ref; commits there are the rigs' actual edits. | short-lived; captured-then-deleted |
70	
71	The codex repo lives in **the same GitHub org as the upstream codex** to
72	keep permission setup simple. Repos are named
73	`experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>` to keep them
74	traceable while alive.
75	
76	### Lifecycle
77	
78	```
79	[run.yaml in sanctum]
80	        │
81	        ▼
82	   run-init        → creates nexus branch (if needed), clones codex into
83	                      a fresh repo, creates guild dir, runs nsg init +
84	                      nsg plugin install + nsg codex add, merges guild
85	                      config, copies input files, writes
86	                      run-status.yaml, stubs run README
87	        │
88	        ▼
89	   [execute run]   → post commissions; rigs execute; sessions record
90	                      into the guild's books
91	        │
92	        ▼
93	   run-archive     → invokes nsg lab export-books to dump the guild's
94	                      books to JSON-per-table; invokes nsg lab git-range
95	                      to capture codex commits between base and HEAD;
96	                      writes status.laboratory.archive
97	        │
98	        ▼
99	   run-cleanup     → deletes codex repo (gh repo delete) and the live
100	                      guild dir; refuses to run if archive wasn't
101	                      completed
102	```
103	
104	The nexus experiment branch is **not** deleted at cleanup — it persists
105	in the nexus repo as a reproducibility breadcrumb.
106	
107	## Run manifest format — `run.yaml`
108	
109	`run.yaml` is the immutable input record for one run. The setup script
110	reads it; nothing should write to it after authoring.
111	
112	```yaml
113	# experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/run.yaml
114	
115	experiment: X<NNN>-<slug>      # experiment slug; matches the parent dir
116	run: NNN                       # run number, zero-padded (001, 002, ...)
117	variant: <variant-slug>        # what makes this run different from others
118	
119	nexus:
120	  branch: experiment/X<NNN>-<slug>     # branch in /workspace/nexus
121	  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # the SHA the branch is expected
122	                                       # to be at; init refuses on drift
123	
124	codex:
125	  upstream_org: shardworks
126	  upstream_repo: nexus                 # or whatever the codex repo is
127	  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # codex SHA the run starts from
128	
129	guild:
130	  plugins:                             # exact plugin pinning for the
131	                                       # experimental package.json. Each
132	                                       # plugin's `version` is whatever
133	                                       # `nsg plugin install` accepts —
134	                                       # an npm semver, a git SHA, a
135	                                       # local path. SHAs from a nexus
136	                                       # experiment branch are the
137	                                       # expected case.
138	    - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
139	      version: <SHA-or-version>
140	    - name: '@shardworks/animator-apparatus'
141	      version: <SHA-or-version>
142	    # ...
143	
144	  config:                              # partial guild.json merged into
145	                                       # the default after `nsg init`.
146	                                       # Free-form; whatever the guild
147	                                       # supports.
148	    <arbitrary partial guild.json>
149	
150	  files:                               # files copied into the guild
151	                                       # after init
152	    - sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md
153	                                       # relative to this run.yaml
154	      guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md
155	                                       # relative to the guild root
156	    # ...
157	```
158	
159	### Schema notes
160	
161	- `experiment` and `variant` slugs use lowercase kebab-case, no whitespace.
162	- `run` is zero-padded to three digits.
163	- All SHAs are full 40-char (no abbreviated forms). The init script
164	  refuses ambiguous refs.
165	- `guild.plugins[].version` is whatever `nsg plugin install` accepts.
166	  Git SHAs are the expected case for nexus-side plugins (so we can
167	  reference unpublished work on experiment branches), but semver
168	  versions or local paths are fine for non-nexus plugins.
169	- `guild.config` and `guild.files` together cover all variant
170	  customization. Anything that used to be a `prompts_dir` is just a
171	  file entry copying into the guild's instruction location.
172	
173	## Run status format — `run-status.yaml`
174	
175	`run-status.yaml` is the mutable record of the run's lifecycle. The
176	init, archive, and cleanup scripts write to it. Status entries are keyed
177	by plugin ID (Nexus convention); the Laboratory plugin owns this
178	infrastructure, so all entries sit under `laboratory`.
179	
180	```yaml
181	# experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/run-status.yaml
182	
183	laboratory:
184	  phase: new                           # new | active | completed | cancelled
185	                                       # default is `new` if omitted
186	
187	  setup:                               # filled at run-init time
188	    timestamp: 2026-...
189	    invocation: 'bin/run-init.sh --manifest .../run.yaml'
190	    nexus:
191	      branch: experiment/X016-...
192	      head_sha: <40-char>              # actual HEAD when init ran
193	    codex:
194	      repo_url: https://github.com/shardworks/experiment-...
195	      head_sha: <40-char>
196	    guild:
197	      path: /workspace/experiments/...
198	      plugins_resolved:                # what was actually installed
199	        - { name: '...', version: '...' }
200	      files_copied:
201	        - { sourcePath: '...', guildPath: '...' }
202	
203	  archive:                             # filled at run-archive time;
204	                                       # overwritten on re-archive (no
205	                                       # preservation of prior runs)
206	    timestamp: 2026-...
207	    invocation: 'bin/run-archive.sh ...'
208	    counts:
209	      tables: <N>
210	      rows: <N>
211	      commits: <N>
212	    paths:
213	      stacks_export: artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/stacks-export/
214	      codex_history: artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/codex-history/
215	```
216	
217	`phase` is advisory — humans (or future scripts) update it as the run
218	progresses. Cleanup refuses based on `archive` being present, not on
219	`phase`.
220	
221	## Setup script — `run-init.sh`
222	
223	```
224	bin/run-init.sh --manifest <path-to-run.yaml>
225	```
226	
227	### Steps
228	
229	1. **Validate manifest.** Required fields present, SHAs are 40-char and
230	   resolve, paths exist, slug not already in use (no existing guild dir
231	   at the target location).
232	2. **Ensure nexus branch.** If the manifest's branch doesn't exist in
233	   `/workspace/nexus`, create it from `nexus.base_sha`. If it exists,
234	   verify it points at the manifest's SHA — refuse to proceed on drift.
235	   Push to origin if newly created.
236	3. **Create the codex repo.** Clone the upstream codex at
237	   `codex.base_sha` into a temp dir, then `gh repo create
238	   <upstream_org>/experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant> --private
239	   --source=<temp-dir> --push`. Not a GitHub fork — a fresh repo seeded
240	   from the upstream SHA. Keeps fork metadata off the upstream codex.
241	4. **Create guild dir** at
242	   `/workspace/experiments/<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>/`.
243	5. **Initialize the guild.** Run `nsg init <guild-dir>` (verify the
244	   exact command surface against framework source).
245	6. **Install plugins.** For each entry in `guild.plugins`:
246	   `nsg plugin install <name>@<version>` against the guild dir.
247	7. **Add the codex.** `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha <codex-base-sha>`
248	   against the guild dir.
249	8. **Merge guild config.** Read `guild.config` from the manifest;
250	   deep-merge into `<guild-dir>/guild.json` (created by `nsg init`).
251	9. **Copy files.** For each entry in `guild.files`, copy `sourcePath`
252	   (resolved relative to `run.yaml`) to `guildPath` (resolved relative
253	   to the guild root). Create intermediate dirs as needed.
254	10. **Write `run-status.yaml`.** Capture every resolved SHA, the
255	    guild path, the codex repo URL, the resolved plugin versions, and
256	    the copied file list under `laboratory.setup`. Set
257	    `laboratory.phase: new`.
258	11. **Generate run README stub** at
259	    `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/README.md`
260	    with the **Setup** section auto-filled from `laboratory.setup` and
261	    the rest as headers waiting for the experiment author to fill in.
262	12. **Print next steps.** "Guild initialized at
263	    `/workspace/experiments/...`. Post commissions via
264	    `nsg commission-post --guild ...`. When the run is done, run
265	    `bin/run-archive.sh <run-slug>` to capture state, then
266	    `bin/run-cleanup.sh <run-slug>` to remove disposable surfaces."
267	
268	### Idempotency
269	
270	Re-running init on an existing guild dir refuses to proceed (with a
271	clear error, no overwrite). Force-re-init is not supported — the
272	intended flow is `archive + cleanup + re-init`, never silent overwrite.
273	
274	## Archive script — `run-archive.sh`
275	
276	```
277	bin/run-archive.sh <run-slug>
278	```
279	
280	Where `<run-slug>` is the form `X<NNN>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`,
281	or a path to the live guild dir. The script resolves to the
282	corresponding sanctum artifact dir.
283	
284	### Steps
285	
286	1. **Read `run-status.yaml`** to find the guild dir, codex repo URL,
287	   and base SHAs.
288	2. **Export the books.** `nsg lab export-books --guild <guild-dir>
289	   --output artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/stacks-export/`. The Laboratory
290	   plugin's tool walks every book and writes JSON-per-table to the
291	   output dir. (Tool to be implemented; see *Implementation
292	   prerequisites*.)
293	3. **Capture codex commits.** `nsg lab git-range --repo <codex-repo>
294	   --base <codex-base-sha> --head <head-sha> --output
295	   artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/codex-history/`. The Laboratory tool
296	   walks the commit range, writes per-commit diffs and a manifest of
297	   metadata. Generic tool, useful beyond this infrastructure.
298	4. **Update `run-status.yaml`.** Write `laboratory.archive` with
299	   timestamp, invocation, counts, and output paths. Overwrites any
300	   prior archive entry.
301	5. **Print next steps.** "Archive complete. Run
302	   `bin/run-cleanup.sh <run-slug>` to delete disposable surfaces.
303	   Verify the archive contents at `<sanctum-path>` before running
304	   cleanup."
305	
306	The script is idempotent: re-running overwrites the prior dump (no
307	preservation of prior archive runs). Cleanup refuses to run if
308	`laboratory.archive` is missing or stale relative to the live state.
309	
310	### Note on transcripts
311	
312	Animator session transcripts are stored in the `sessions` book by
313	the Animator plugin, so they come along automatically with
314	`export-books`. No separate transcript-copy step. This sidesteps both
315	the `~/.claude/projects/` dependency and the multi-provider question
316	(any provider Animator supports lands its transcripts in the same
317	book).
318	
319	## Cleanup script — `run-cleanup.sh`
320	
321	```
322	bin/run-cleanup.sh <run-slug>
323	```
324	
325	### Steps
326	
327	1. **Verify archive completion.** Refuse to run if
328	   `run-status.yaml` lacks a `laboratory.archive` entry, or if its
329	   timestamp is older than the guild dir's most recent modification
330	   (suggests work happened after archive — re-archive before cleanup).
331	2. **Delete the codex repo.** `gh repo delete <repo-url> --yes`. Fail
332	   loud if the repo has unmerged content the script doesn't recognize
333	   from the archive (rare, but the safety check matters).
334	3. **Delete the guild dir.** `rm -rf /workspace/experiments/<run-dir>`.
335	4. **Print confirmation.** "Run <slug> cleanup complete. Sanctum
336	   artifacts preserved at `<path>`."
337	
338	The script is **not idempotent** — re-running on a cleaned run errors
339	("nothing to clean up").
340	
341	## Artifact layout
342	
343	```
344	experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/
345	├── spec.md                                # the experiment's research spec
346	├── findings.md                            # cross-run consolidated findings
347	└── artifacts/
348	    ├── run-001-<variant-slug>/
349	    │   ├── run.yaml                       # immutable inputs
350	    │   ├── run-status.yaml                # mutable status (phase, setup, archive)
351	    │   ├── README.md                      # run-level prose doc
352	    │   ├── files/                         # variant-specific source files
353	    │   │   └── ...                        #   (whatever guild.files entries source)
354	    │   ├── stacks-export/                 # nsg lab export-books output
355	    │   │   ├── writs.json
356	    │   │   ├── rigs.json
357	    │   │   ├── sessions.json              # transcripts live here
358	    │   │   ├── clicks.json
359	    │   │   └── schema-version.txt
360	    │   ├── codex-history/                 # nsg lab git-range output
361	    │   │   ├── commits-manifest.yaml
362	    │   │   └── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
363	    │   └── analysis/                      # run-specific analysis (if any)
364	    │       └── <date>-<slug>.md
365	    │
366	    ├── run-002-<variant-slug>/            # parallel structure per run
367	    │   └── ...
368	    │
369	    └── analysis/                          # cross-run analysis
370	        └── <date>-comparison.md
371	```
372	
373	### Notes
374	
375	- **Run dir name is `run-NNN-<variant-slug>`.** NNN is zero-padded;
376	  variant slug is lowercase kebab-case.
377	- **`run-NNN-<variant>/analysis/`** is for analysis specific to one
378	  run; **`artifacts/analysis/`** is for cross-run comparison.
379	- **Findings go in `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/findings.md`** at the
380	  experiment root. The findings doc consolidates across runs and
381	  answers the experiment's primary research question.
382	
383	## Stacks export format
384	
385	One JSON file per book/table, each containing an array of row objects.
386	Produced by `nsg lab export-books`.
387	
388	```json
389	[
390	  {
391	    "id": "w-...",
392	    "type": "mandate",
393	    "title": "...",
394	    "body": "...",
395	    "status": "open",
396	    "createdAt": "2026-...",
397	    "...": "..."
398	  },
399	  { "id": "w-...", "...": "..." }
400	]
401	```
402	
403	### File-per-table choice
404	
405	Per-table beats per-row for our use case:
406	
407	- Typical row sizes are 1-10KB; per-row overhead (filesystem blocks,
408	  git blob entries) eats meaningful space at this scale.
409	- Row counts per run are tens to hundreds — table files stay
410	  manageable.
411	- We don't need row-level git history (dumps are snapshots, not
412	  append-only).
413	- Analysis instruments load the table once and iterate.
414	
415	### Carve-out for large content
416	
417	If any single row carries content >50KB (e.g., a writ body that's a
418	50KB brief), the dump can extract it to `stacks-export/blobs/<sha>.<ext>`
419	and replace the inline content with a reference field
420	(`body_ref: "blobs/<sha>.md"`). Only do this when actually needed; the
421	default is inline. Session-transcript content in `sessions.json` will
422	typically trigger this carve-out.
423	
424	### Schema versioning
425	
426	`stacks-export/schema-version.txt` records the framework SHA the dump
427	was taken against. Future readers use this to interpret the row schema.
428	
429	### Default scope
430	
431	A fresh guild contains only the run's data, so the default dump is
432	**every book, every row**. No scope filtering needed.
433	
434	## Codex history format
435	
436	Produced by `nsg lab git-range`.
437	
438	```
439	codex-history/
440	├── commits-manifest.yaml
441	├── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
442	├── 0002-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
443	└── ...
444	```
445	
446	### `commits-manifest.yaml`
447	
448	```yaml
449	base_sha: <40-char>            # the run's codex base
450	head_sha: <40-char>            # the repo's main HEAD at archive time
451	commits:
452	  - sha: <40-char>
453	    parent: <40-char>
454	    short: <8-char>
455	    subject: 'reckoner: sweep vision-keeper references'
456	    author: 'implementer'
457	    timestamp: '2026-...'
458	    diff_file: '0001-<short>-<slug>.diff'
459	  - ...
460	```
461	
462	The list is order-preserving (oldest first). No `seq` field — YAML
463	sequence position carries it. The tool is generic — no run-specific
464	cross-references in the manifest. Cross-references between commits and
465	guild objects (rig, session, writ) live in the books export, not here.
466	The diff file's `0001-` prefix on disk preserves order for filesystem
467	listing.
468	
469	### Diff files
470	
471	Standard `git format-patch` output; one file per commit. The filename's
472	numeric prefix preserves order; the short SHA + slug aids human
473	navigation.
474	
475	## Run README format
476	
477	Each run dir has a `README.md` capturing the run's narrative.
478	
479	```markdown
480	# Run <NNN> — <variant slug>
481	
482	## What this run tests
483	<one paragraph: which variable settings; what we're hoping to observe>
484	
485	## Setup
486	- Nexus branch: <branch> @ <SHA>
487	- Codex repo: <URL> @ <SHA>
488	- Plugin deps: see `run-status.yaml`
489	- Variant config: <pointer to files/ or guild.config in run.yaml>
490	
491	## Methodology
492	<what commissions were posted, in what order, what was measured>
493	
494	## Results
495	<metrics, comparisons, surprises; reference analysis/ files>
496	
497	## Verdict
498	<answer to the run's question, with a one-paragraph rationale>
499	```
500	
501	The init script auto-fills **Setup** with resolved SHAs and paths. The
502	experiment author fills the rest.
503	
504	## Experiment discipline
505	
506	An experiment is one piece of inquiry — a research question plus the
507	analytical frame to answer it. An experiment produces one or more
508	runs, each capturing what happened when the apparatus was set up
509	and executed with a specific set of variable settings.
510	
511	### Experiments
512	
513	- **One experiment has one primary research question** — the verdict
514	  it must answer. The findings doc resolves to that question.
515	- **Secondary findings are allowed and welcome** — analyses of the
516	  same captured data that illuminate other questions. Label them as
517	  secondary; they don't dilute the verdict.
518	- **No HARKing.** New primary questions that emerge during analysis
519	  become new experiments with their own pre-registered question.
520	  Don't retrofit a question to fit data you've already seen.
521	- **Number of runs per experiment is whatever the analysis needs.**
522	  Could be one (qualitative, observational) or many (varying
523	  parameters, replicating).
524	- **Old X### items with multiple primary questions** stay as
525	  historical exceptions. Don't rationalize. Apply the discipline
526	  going forward.
527	
528	### Runs
529	
530	- **A run is a single execution of the apparatus** with a specific set
531	  of variable settings. It produces a captured-state artifact set in
532	  `run-NNN-<variant>/`.
533	- **Variables split into controlled and manipulated.** Controlled
534	  variables are held constant across runs. Manipulated variables are
535	  what each run varies. Both go in `run.yaml` — there's no special
536	  field marking which is which; the variation across an experiment's
537	  runs makes that visible.
538	- **Replicates are runs with identical variable settings** — useful
539	  when checking for non-determinism or noise. Treat as ordinary runs
540	  with a `replicate-of` field if needed; don't engineer special
541	  structure now.
542	
543	## Naming conventions
544	
545	| thing | format | example |
546	|---|---|---|
547	| Experiment slug | `X<NNN>-<lowercase-kebab>` | `X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
548	| Nexus experiment branch | `experiment/<experiment-slug>` (per-run variants ok) | `experiment/X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
549	| Codex repo name | `experiment-<experiment-slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>` | `experiment-X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt` |
550	| Run dir slug | `<experiment-slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>` | (used in `/workspace/experiments/` and as the cleanup arg) |
551	| Run artifact dir | `run-<NNN>-<variant>/` | `run-001-strong-prompt/` |
552	
553	Variant slugs use lowercase kebab-case, descriptive but short.
554	
555	## Worked example: X016 P3 orientation suppression
556	
557	(Hypothetical — illustrates how the pieces fit together.)
558	
559	### Experiment
560	
561	- **Number:** X016
562	- **Slug:** `X016-p3-orientation-suppression`
563	- **Primary research question:** Can a fresh implementer session, given
564	  a hand-crafted 30K-token handoff, produce productive work in <5 turns?
565	- **Spec location:** `experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/spec.md`
566	
567	### Runs
568	
569	Three prompt variants tested against the same brief (rig 1's docs sweep,
570	handoff at T52). Common controlled variables: nexus SHA, codex base SHA,
571	plugin versions. Manipulated: the handoff prompt content (and presence).
572	
573	| run | variant | what differs |
574	|---|---|---|
575	| 001 | `strong-prompt` | imperative anti-orientation directives ("Do NOT re-read X"), evidence anchors |
576	| 002 | `mild-prompt` | permissive framing ("X is known-good") |
577	| 003 | `monolithic-baseline` | no handoff; runs the brief from scratch as control |
578	
579	### Per-run flow
580	
581	1. Author writes `run.yaml` for run-001-strong-prompt, including the
582	   shared nexus + codex SHAs and the variant-specific
583	   `guild.files: [{ sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md,
584	   guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md }]`.
585	2. `bin/run-init.sh --manifest .../run-001-strong-prompt/run.yaml`
586	   creates the nexus branch (if not already), creates the codex repo,
587	   inits the guild via `nsg init`, installs plugins via `nsg plugin
588	   install`, adds the codex via `nsg codex add`, merges
589	   `guild.config`, copies the handoff prompt into place, writes
590	   `run-status.yaml`, stubs the README.
591	3. Post the brief commission via `nsg commission-post --guild
592	   /workspace/experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt
593	   --brief .../files/brief-docs-sweep.md`.
594	4. Rig executes; sessions record into the guild's books.
595	5. When the run completes, `bin/run-archive.sh
596	   X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt` invokes
597	   `nsg lab export-books` (dumps books) and `nsg lab git-range`
598	   (captures codex commits), updates `run-status.yaml`.
599	6. Author fills out the run README with methodology + results +
600	   verdict.
601	7. `bin/run-cleanup.sh X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt`
602	   deletes the codex repo and guild dir.
603	8. Repeat for runs 002 and 003.
604	
605	### Cross-run analysis
606	
607	`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/artifacts/analysis/2026-MM-DD-orientation-comparison.md`
608	runs an instrument that compares orientation-tax metrics across the
609	three runs' books exports and codex histories; produces the answer to
610	the primary research question. Findings consolidate to
611	`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/findings.md`.
612	
613	## Implementation prerequisites
614	
615	These are pieces that need to exist (or be verified) before the
616	infrastructure scripts can be built:
617	
618	1. **Verify `nsg init` invocation.** Inspect framework source to
619	   confirm the exact command surface — does it accept a target dir
620	   argument, where does it create `guild.json`, etc.
621	2. **Verify `nsg plugin install` invocation.** Confirm the version
622	   spec accepts SHAs / git refs (not just npm semver). If not,
623	   negotiate with framework before scripts can pin to experiment
624	   branches.
625	3. **Verify `nsg codex add` invocation.** Confirm the SHA-pinning
626	   surface.
627	4. **Build `nsg lab export-books`** — Laboratory tool that takes a
628	   guild dir and an output dir and writes JSON-per-table for every
629	   book. Promotion to the `stacks` plugin if it proves itself.
630	5. **Build `nsg lab git-range`** — Laboratory tool that takes a repo
631	   path, base SHA, head SHA, and output dir and writes the
632	   commits-manifest + per-commit diff files. Generic tool, useful
633	   beyond this infrastructure.
634	6. **Confirm `gh` org permissions.** Assume the experimental setup
635	   user has permission to create / delete repos in the upstream codex
636	   org. If that turns out wrong at script time, surface and adjust.
637	
638	## Future considerations
639	
640	- **Multi-run experiments running in parallel.** The current spec
641	  defaults to sequential runs. Some experiments may want concurrent
642	  runs to save calendar time. Design is compatible — just init
643	  multiple `run.yaml`s; runs are isolated by construction.
644	- **Long-lived runs.** Some runs may execute over weeks (e.g.,
645	  observing P3 behavior across many real commissions). The cleanup
646	  discipline needs adjusting for these — possibly an interim
647	  "snapshot" step that captures state without deleting the codex repo.
648	- **Cross-experiment comparison.** When two experiments answer related
649	  questions (e.g., X016 P3 orientation, X017 P3 trigger heuristics),
650	  cross-experiment analysis lives in `experiments/analysis/` (or
651	  wherever the sanctum convention places it). Out of scope for this
652	  spec.
653	- **Replication discipline.** If we start running replicates (same
654	  variable settings, multiple runs to check for noise), the `run.yaml`
655	  schema gains a `replicate-of: <run-NNN>` field. Defer until needed.
656

file.content:
# Experimental infrastructure: setup, artifacts, lifecycle

**Status:** draft (2026-04-30) — refined with Sean's feedback.

## Purpose

This document specifies the infrastructure for running framework-side
experiments — runs that need a controlled codex, controlled framework
state, isolated session recording, and reproducible setup. The immediate
driver is P3 (engine-pipeline-decomposition; click `c-modxxtu6`), whose
sub-questions (orientation suppression, trigger heuristics, granularity,
cost confirmation) all require executing real implementer sessions in
isolated conditions before the architecture can be designed empirically.
Subsequent experiments touching framework or commission-pipeline behavior
will reuse the same infrastructure.

The infrastructure does **not** govern paper analyses (instruments run
against existing transcript archives) — those need no setup. It governs
work that **executes new commissions** under controlled conditions.

## Vocabulary

- **Experiment.** One piece of inquiry — a research question plus the
  analytical frame to answer it. Lives at
  `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/`.
- **Run.** One execution of the experimental apparatus with a specific
  set of variable settings. An experiment produces one or more runs.
  Lives at `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/`.
- **Variables.** The inputs that define a run. Split into **controlled**
  (held constant — framework SHA, codex base, common config) and
  **manipulated** (varied per run — prompts, plugin versions, config
  fragments). Both kinds live in `run.yaml`.

## Design principles

1. **Three-repo separation.** The sanctum, the framework (nexus), and the
   codex live in three repos with distinct persistence rules. Each artifact
   has one canonical home.
2. **One guild per run.** Every run gets its own fresh guild and its
   own short-lived codex repo. This eliminates run-isolation problems
   inside the guild's books — every row in the guild DB belongs to this
   run by construction.
3. **Sanctum is the canonical record.** Methodology, manifests, input
   files, captured execution data, and analysis live in the sanctum. The
   other two repos (nexus branches, codex repos) are reproducibility
   breadcrumbs.
4. **Reproducibility via SHA pinning.** Every framework dep, every codex
   base, every plugin version is pinned to a SHA in a per-run manifest
   that lives in the sanctum.
5. **Disposable execution surfaces.** Codex repos and live guild dirs are
   short-lived. They're created at run start, archived to sanctum at
   run end, and deleted. Re-running an experiment doesn't depend on
   long-lived disposable state.
6. **Standard guild mechanisms over bespoke generation.** The setup
   script composes existing `nsg` commands (`nsg init`, `nsg plugin
   install`, `nsg codex add`) and Laboratory tools rather than
   hand-generating `guild.json` / `package.json`. Custom guild
   configuration is expressed as a partial `guild.json` merge plus a
   list of files to copy.

## Architecture

### The three repos

| repo | role | persistence |
|---|---|---|
| **Sanctum** (`nexus-mk2`) | methodology, manifests, input files, captured artifacts, analysis, findings | indefinite; canonical research record |
| **Nexus framework** | the framework state that produces experimental variance. Each run declares whatever branch + base SHA it needs. Runs that share framework state share a branch; runs that introduce framework variance get their own. No enforced cardinality. | indefinite; archived branches |
| **Codex repo** | short-lived fresh repo (clone of the upstream codex, pushed as a new repo — *not* a GitHub fork, to avoid leaving fork metadata on the upstream). One per run. The repo's `main` branch is the run's working ref; commits there are the rigs' actual edits. | short-lived; captured-then-deleted |

The codex repo lives in **the same GitHub org as the upstream codex** to
keep permission setup simple. Repos are named
`experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>` to keep them
traceable while alive.

### Lifecycle

```
[run.yaml in sanctum]
        │
        ▼
   run-init        → creates nexus branch (if needed), clones codex into
                      a fresh repo, creates guild dir, runs nsg init +
                      nsg plugin install + nsg codex add, merges guild
                      config, copies input files, writes
                      run-status.yaml, stubs run README
        │
        ▼
   [execute run]   → post commissions; rigs execute; sessions record
                      into the guild's books
        │
        ▼
   run-archive     → invokes nsg lab export-books to dump the guild's
                      books to JSON-per-table; invokes nsg lab git-range
                      to capture codex commits between base and HEAD;
                      writes status.laboratory.archive
        │
        ▼
   run-cleanup     → deletes codex repo (gh repo delete) and the live
                      guild dir; refuses to run if archive wasn't
                      completed
```

The nexus experiment branch is **not** deleted at cleanup — it persists
in the nexus repo as a reproducibility breadcrumb.

## Run manifest format — `run.yaml`

`run.yaml` is the immutable input record for one run. The setup script
reads it; nothing should write to it after authoring.

```yaml
# experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/run.yaml

experiment: X<NNN>-<slug>      # experiment slug; matches the parent dir
run: NNN                       # run number, zero-padded (001, 002, ...)
variant: <variant-slug>        # what makes this run different from others

nexus:
  branch: experiment/X<NNN>-<slug>     # branch in /workspace/nexus
  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # the SHA the branch is expected
                                       # to be at; init refuses on drift

codex:
  upstream_org: shardworks
  upstream_repo: nexus                 # or whatever the codex repo is
  base_sha: <40-char SHA>              # codex SHA the run starts from

guild:
  plugins:                             # exact plugin pinning for the
                                       # experimental package.json. Each
                                       # plugin's `version` is whatever
                                       # `nsg plugin install` accepts —
                                       # an npm semver, a git SHA, a
                                       # local path. SHAs from a nexus
                                       # experiment branch are the
                                       # expected case.
    - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
      version: <SHA-or-version>
    - name: '@shardworks/animator-apparatus'
      version: <SHA-or-version>
    # ...

  config:                              # partial guild.json merged into
                                       # the default after `nsg init`.
                                       # Free-form; whatever the guild
                                       # supports.
    <arbitrary partial guild.json>

  files:                               # files copied into the guild
                                       # after init
    - sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md
                                       # relative to this run.yaml
      guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md
                                       # relative to the guild root
    # ...
```

### Schema notes

- `experiment` and `variant` slugs use lowercase kebab-case, no whitespace.
- `run` is zero-padded to three digits.
- All SHAs are full 40-char (no abbreviated forms). The init script
  refuses ambiguous refs.
- `guild.plugins[].version` is whatever `nsg plugin install` accepts.
  Git SHAs are the expected case for nexus-side plugins (so we can
  reference unpublished work on experiment branches), but semver
  versions or local paths are fine for non-nexus plugins.
- `guild.config` and `guild.files` together cover all variant
  customization. Anything that used to be a `prompts_dir` is just a
  file entry copying into the guild's instruction location.

## Run status format — `run-status.yaml`

`run-status.yaml` is the mutable record of the run's lifecycle. The
init, archive, and cleanup scripts write to it. Status entries are keyed
by plugin ID (Nexus convention); the Laboratory plugin owns this
infrastructure, so all entries sit under `laboratory`.

```yaml
# experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/run-status.yaml

laboratory:
  phase: new                           # new | active | completed | cancelled
                                       # default is `new` if omitted

  setup:                               # filled at run-init time
    timestamp: 2026-...
    invocation: 'bin/run-init.sh --manifest .../run.yaml'
    nexus:
      branch: experiment/X016-...
      head_sha: <40-char>              # actual HEAD when init ran
    codex:
      repo_url: https://github.com/shardworks/experiment-...
      head_sha: <40-char>
    guild:
      path: /workspace/experiments/...
      plugins_resolved:                # what was actually installed
        - { name: '...', version: '...' }
      files_copied:
        - { sourcePath: '...', guildPath: '...' }

  archive:                             # filled at run-archive time;
                                       # overwritten on re-archive (no
                                       # preservation of prior runs)
    timestamp: 2026-...
    invocation: 'bin/run-archive.sh ...'
    counts:
      tables: <N>
      rows: <N>
      commits: <N>
    paths:
      stacks_export: artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/stacks-export/
      codex_history: artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/codex-history/
```

`phase` is advisory — humans (or future scripts) update it as the run
progresses. Cleanup refuses based on `archive` being present, not on
`phase`.

## Setup script — `run-init.sh`

```
bin/run-init.sh --manifest <path-to-run.yaml>
```

### Steps

1. **Validate manifest.** Required fields present, SHAs are 40-char and
   resolve, paths exist, slug not already in use (no existing guild dir
   at the target location).
2. **Ensure nexus branch.** If the manifest's branch doesn't exist in
   `/workspace/nexus`, create it from `nexus.base_sha`. If it exists,
   verify it points at the manifest's SHA — refuse to proceed on drift.
   Push to origin if newly created.
3. **Create the codex repo.** Clone the upstream codex at
   `codex.base_sha` into a temp dir, then `gh repo create
   <upstream_org>/experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant> --private
   --source=<temp-dir> --push`. Not a GitHub fork — a fresh repo seeded
   from the upstream SHA. Keeps fork metadata off the upstream codex.
4. **Create guild dir** at
   `/workspace/experiments/<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>/`.
5. **Initialize the guild.** Run `nsg init <guild-dir>` (verify the
   exact command surface against framework source).
6. **Install plugins.** For each entry in `guild.plugins`:
   `nsg plugin install <name>@<version>` against the guild dir.
7. **Add the codex.** `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha <codex-base-sha>`
   against the guild dir.
8. **Merge guild config.** Read `guild.config` from the manifest;
   deep-merge into `<guild-dir>/guild.json` (created by `nsg init`).
9. **Copy files.** For each entry in `guild.files`, copy `sourcePath`
   (resolved relative to `run.yaml`) to `guildPath` (resolved relative
   to the guild root). Create intermediate dirs as needed.
10. **Write `run-status.yaml`.** Capture every resolved SHA, the
    guild path, the codex repo URL, the resolved plugin versions, and
    the copied file list under `laboratory.setup`. Set
    `laboratory.phase: new`.
11. **Generate run README stub** at
    `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/README.md`
    with the **Setup** section auto-filled from `laboratory.setup` and
    the rest as headers waiting for the experiment author to fill in.
12. **Print next steps.** "Guild initialized at
    `/workspace/experiments/...`. Post commissions via
    `nsg commission-post --guild ...`. When the run is done, run
    `bin/run-archive.sh <run-slug>` to capture state, then
    `bin/run-cleanup.sh <run-slug>` to remove disposable surfaces."

### Idempotency

Re-running init on an existing guild dir refuses to proceed (with a
clear error, no overwrite). Force-re-init is not supported — the
intended flow is `archive + cleanup + re-init`, never silent overwrite.

## Archive script — `run-archive.sh`

```
bin/run-archive.sh <run-slug>
```

Where `<run-slug>` is the form `X<NNN>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`,
or a path to the live guild dir. The script resolves to the
corresponding sanctum artifact dir.

### Steps

1. **Read `run-status.yaml`** to find the guild dir, codex repo URL,
   and base SHAs.
2. **Export the books.** `nsg lab export-books --guild <guild-dir>
   --output artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/stacks-export/`. The Laboratory
   plugin's tool walks every book and writes JSON-per-table to the
   output dir. (Tool to be implemented; see *Implementation
   prerequisites*.)
3. **Capture codex commits.** `nsg lab git-range --repo <codex-repo>
   --base <codex-base-sha> --head <head-sha> --output
   artifacts/run-NNN-<variant>/codex-history/`. The Laboratory tool
   walks the commit range, writes per-commit diffs and a manifest of
   metadata. Generic tool, useful beyond this infrastructure.
4. **Update `run-status.yaml`.** Write `laboratory.archive` with
   timestamp, invocation, counts, and output paths. Overwrites any
   prior archive entry.
5. **Print next steps.** "Archive complete. Run
   `bin/run-cleanup.sh <run-slug>` to delete disposable surfaces.
   Verify the archive contents at `<sanctum-path>` before running
   cleanup."

The script is idempotent: re-running overwrites the prior dump (no
preservation of prior archive runs). Cleanup refuses to run if
`laboratory.archive` is missing or stale relative to the live state.

### Note on transcripts

Animator session transcripts are stored in the `sessions` book by
the Animator plugin, so they come along automatically with
`export-books`. No separate transcript-copy step. This sidesteps both
the `~/.claude/projects/` dependency and the multi-provider question
(any provider Animator supports lands its transcripts in the same
book).

## Cleanup script — `run-cleanup.sh`

```
bin/run-cleanup.sh <run-slug>
```

### Steps

1. **Verify archive completion.** Refuse to run if
   `run-status.yaml` lacks a `laboratory.archive` entry, or if its
   timestamp is older than the guild dir's most recent modification
   (suggests work happened after archive — re-archive before cleanup).
2. **Delete the codex repo.** `gh repo delete <repo-url> --yes`. Fail
   loud if the repo has unmerged content the script doesn't recognize
   from the archive (rare, but the safety check matters).
3. **Delete the guild dir.** `rm -rf /workspace/experiments/<run-dir>`.
4. **Print confirmation.** "Run <slug> cleanup complete. Sanctum
   artifacts preserved at `<path>`."

The script is **not idempotent** — re-running on a cleaned run errors
("nothing to clean up").

## Artifact layout

```
experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/
├── spec.md                                # the experiment's research spec
├── findings.md                            # cross-run consolidated findings
└── artifacts/
    ├── run-001-<variant-slug>/
    │   ├── run.yaml                       # immutable inputs
    │   ├── run-status.yaml                # mutable status (phase, setup, archive)
    │   ├── README.md                      # run-level prose doc
    │   ├── files/                         # variant-specific source files
    │   │   └── ...                        #   (whatever guild.files entries source)
    │   ├── stacks-export/                 # nsg lab export-books output
    │   │   ├── writs.json
    │   │   ├── rigs.json
    │   │   ├── sessions.json              # transcripts live here
    │   │   ├── clicks.json
    │   │   └── schema-version.txt
    │   ├── codex-history/                 # nsg lab git-range output
    │   │   ├── commits-manifest.yaml
    │   │   └── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
    │   └── analysis/                      # run-specific analysis (if any)
    │       └── <date>-<slug>.md
    │
    ├── run-002-<variant-slug>/            # parallel structure per run
    │   └── ...
    │
    └── analysis/                          # cross-run analysis
        └── <date>-comparison.md
```

### Notes

- **Run dir name is `run-NNN-<variant-slug>`.** NNN is zero-padded;
  variant slug is lowercase kebab-case.
- **`run-NNN-<variant>/analysis/`** is for analysis specific to one
  run; **`artifacts/analysis/`** is for cross-run comparison.
- **Findings go in `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/findings.md`** at the
  experiment root. The findings doc consolidates across runs and
  answers the experiment's primary research question.

## Stacks export format

One JSON file per book/table, each containing an array of row objects.
Produced by `nsg lab export-books`.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "w-...",
    "type": "mandate",
    "title": "...",
    "body": "...",
    "status": "open",
    "createdAt": "2026-...",
    "...": "..."
  },
  { "id": "w-...", "...": "..." }
]
```

### File-per-table choice

Per-table beats per-row for our use case:

- Typical row sizes are 1-10KB; per-row overhead (filesystem blocks,
  git blob entries) eats meaningful space at this scale.
- Row counts per run are tens to hundreds — table files stay
  manageable.
- We don't need row-level git history (dumps are snapshots, not
  append-only).
- Analysis instruments load the table once and iterate.

### Carve-out for large content

If any single row carries content >50KB (e.g., a writ body that's a
50KB brief), the dump can extract it to `stacks-export/blobs/<sha>.<ext>`
and replace the inline content with a reference field
(`body_ref: "blobs/<sha>.md"`). Only do this when actually needed; the
default is inline. Session-transcript content in `sessions.json` will
typically trigger this carve-out.

### Schema versioning

`stacks-export/schema-version.txt` records the framework SHA the dump
was taken against. Future readers use this to interpret the row schema.

### Default scope

A fresh guild contains only the run's data, so the default dump is
**every book, every row**. No scope filtering needed.

## Codex history format

Produced by `nsg lab git-range`.

```
codex-history/
├── commits-manifest.yaml
├── 0001-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
├── 0002-<short-sha>-<slug>.diff
└── ...
```

### `commits-manifest.yaml`

```yaml
base_sha: <40-char>            # the run's codex base
head_sha: <40-char>            # the repo's main HEAD at archive time
commits:
  - sha: <40-char>
    parent: <40-char>
    short: <8-char>
    subject: 'reckoner: sweep vision-keeper references'
    author: 'implementer'
    timestamp: '2026-...'
    diff_file: '0001-<short>-<slug>.diff'
  - ...
```

The list is order-preserving (oldest first). No `seq` field — YAML
sequence position carries it. The tool is generic — no run-specific
cross-references in the manifest. Cross-references between commits and
guild objects (rig, session, writ) live in the books export, not here.
The diff file's `0001-` prefix on disk preserves order for filesystem
listing.

### Diff files

Standard `git format-patch` output; one file per commit. The filename's
numeric prefix preserves order; the short SHA + slug aids human
navigation.

## Run README format

Each run dir has a `README.md` capturing the run's narrative.

```markdown
# Run <NNN> — <variant slug>

## What this run tests
<one paragraph: which variable settings; what we're hoping to observe>

## Setup
- Nexus branch: <branch> @ <SHA>
- Codex repo: <URL> @ <SHA>
- Plugin deps: see `run-status.yaml`
- Variant config: <pointer to files/ or guild.config in run.yaml>

## Methodology
<what commissions were posted, in what order, what was measured>

## Results
<metrics, comparisons, surprises; reference analysis/ files>

## Verdict
<answer to the run's question, with a one-paragraph rationale>
```

The init script auto-fills **Setup** with resolved SHAs and paths. The
experiment author fills the rest.

## Experiment discipline

An experiment is one piece of inquiry — a research question plus the
analytical frame to answer it. An experiment produces one or more
runs, each capturing what happened when the apparatus was set up
and executed with a specific set of variable settings.

### Experiments

- **One experiment has one primary research question** — the verdict
  it must answer. The findings doc resolves to that question.
- **Secondary findings are allowed and welcome** — analyses of the
  same captured data that illuminate other questions. Label them as
  secondary; they don't dilute the verdict.
- **No HARKing.** New primary questions that emerge during analysis
  become new experiments with their own pre-registered question.
  Don't retrofit a question to fit data you've already seen.
- **Number of runs per experiment is whatever the analysis needs.**
  Could be one (qualitative, observational) or many (varying
  parameters, replicating).
- **Old X### items with multiple primary questions** stay as
  historical exceptions. Don't rationalize. Apply the discipline
  going forward.

### Runs

- **A run is a single execution of the apparatus** with a specific set
  of variable settings. It produces a captured-state artifact set in
  `run-NNN-<variant>/`.
- **Variables split into controlled and manipulated.** Controlled
  variables are held constant across runs. Manipulated variables are
  what each run varies. Both go in `run.yaml` — there's no special
  field marking which is which; the variation across an experiment's
  runs makes that visible.
- **Replicates are runs with identical variable settings** — useful
  when checking for non-determinism or noise. Treat as ordinary runs
  with a `replicate-of` field if needed; don't engineer special
  structure now.

## Naming conventions

| thing | format | example |
|---|---|---|
| Experiment slug | `X<NNN>-<lowercase-kebab>` | `X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
| Nexus experiment branch | `experiment/<experiment-slug>` (per-run variants ok) | `experiment/X016-p3-orientation-suppression` |
| Codex repo name | `experiment-<experiment-slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>` | `experiment-X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt` |
| Run dir slug | `<experiment-slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>` | (used in `/workspace/experiments/` and as the cleanup arg) |
| Run artifact dir | `run-<NNN>-<variant>/` | `run-001-strong-prompt/` |

Variant slugs use lowercase kebab-case, descriptive but short.

## Worked example: X016 P3 orientation suppression

(Hypothetical — illustrates how the pieces fit together.)

### Experiment

- **Number:** X016
- **Slug:** `X016-p3-orientation-suppression`
- **Primary research question:** Can a fresh implementer session, given
  a hand-crafted 30K-token handoff, produce productive work in <5 turns?
- **Spec location:** `experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/spec.md`

### Runs

Three prompt variants tested against the same brief (rig 1's docs sweep,
handoff at T52). Common controlled variables: nexus SHA, codex base SHA,
plugin versions. Manipulated: the handoff prompt content (and presence).

| run | variant | what differs |
|---|---|---|
| 001 | `strong-prompt` | imperative anti-orientation directives ("Do NOT re-read X"), evidence anchors |
| 002 | `mild-prompt` | permissive framing ("X is known-good") |
| 003 | `monolithic-baseline` | no handoff; runs the brief from scratch as control |

### Per-run flow

1. Author writes `run.yaml` for run-001-strong-prompt, including the
   shared nexus + codex SHAs and the variant-specific
   `guild.files: [{ sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md,
   guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md }]`.
2. `bin/run-init.sh --manifest .../run-001-strong-prompt/run.yaml`
   creates the nexus branch (if not already), creates the codex repo,
   inits the guild via `nsg init`, installs plugins via `nsg plugin
   install`, adds the codex via `nsg codex add`, merges
   `guild.config`, copies the handoff prompt into place, writes
   `run-status.yaml`, stubs the README.
3. Post the brief commission via `nsg commission-post --guild
   /workspace/experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt
   --brief .../files/brief-docs-sweep.md`.
4. Rig executes; sessions record into the guild's books.
5. When the run completes, `bin/run-archive.sh
   X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt` invokes
   `nsg lab export-books` (dumps books) and `nsg lab git-range`
   (captures codex commits), updates `run-status.yaml`.
6. Author fills out the run README with methodology + results +
   verdict.
7. `bin/run-cleanup.sh X016-p3-orientation-suppression-run-001-strong-prompt`
   deletes the codex repo and guild dir.
8. Repeat for runs 002 and 003.

### Cross-run analysis

`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/artifacts/analysis/2026-MM-DD-orientation-comparison.md`
runs an instrument that compares orientation-tax metrics across the
three runs' books exports and codex histories; produces the answer to
the primary research question. Findings consolidate to
`experiments/X016-p3-orientation-suppression/findings.md`.

## Implementation prerequisites

These are pieces that need to exist (or be verified) before the
infrastructure scripts can be built:

1. **Verify `nsg init` invocation.** Inspect framework source to
   confirm the exact command surface — does it accept a target dir
   argument, where does it create `guild.json`, etc.
2. **Verify `nsg plugin install` invocation.** Confirm the version
   spec accepts SHAs / git refs (not just npm semver). If not,
   negotiate with framework before scripts can pin to experiment
   branches.
3. **Verify `nsg codex add` invocation.** Confirm the SHA-pinning
   surface.
4. **Build `nsg lab export-books`** — Laboratory tool that takes a
   guild dir and an output dir and writes JSON-per-table for every
   book. Promotion to the `stacks` plugin if it proves itself.
5. **Build `nsg lab git-range`** — Laboratory tool that takes a repo
   path, base SHA, head SHA, and output dir and writes the
   commits-manifest + per-commit diff files. Generic tool, useful
   beyond this infrastructure.
6. **Confirm `gh` org permissions.** Assume the experimental setup
   user has permission to create / delete repos in the upstream codex
   org. If that turns out wrong at script time, surface and adjust.

## Future considerations

- **Multi-run experiments running in parallel.** The current spec
  defaults to sequential runs. Some experiments may want concurrent
  runs to save calendar time. Design is compatible — just init
  multiple `run.yaml`s; runs are isolated by construction.
- **Long-lived runs.** Some runs may execute over weeks (e.g.,
  observing P3 behavior across many real commissions). The cleanup
  discipline needs adjusting for these — possibly an interim
  "snapshot" step that captures state without deleting the codex repo.
- **Cross-experiment comparison.** When two experiments answer related
  questions (e.g., X016 P3 orientation, X017 P3 trigger heuristics),
  cross-experiment analysis lives in `experiments/analysis/` (or
  wherever the sanctum convention places it). Out of scope for this
  spec.
- **Replication discipline.** If we start running replicates (same
  variable settings, multiple runs to check for noise), the `run.yaml`
  schema gains a `replicate-of: <run-NNN>` field. Defer until needed.

content.text:
Got it — re-framing acknowledged. The Laboratory plugin lives at `/workspace/nexus-mk2/packages/laboratory/` (currently the no-op stub), is `pnpm link`-ed into vibers, and is sanctum-owned. Coco builds it directly. No briefs, no commissions, no autonomous dispatch.

Quick scan to make sure I have the right picture:

- Sanctum package exists, vibers guild already registers it as `laboratory` and links `@shardworks/laboratory-apparatus` to `../nexus-mk2/packages/laboratory`. So edits land in the running guild after a rebuild + restart.
- Sibling sanctum package `packages/instruments/` exists too — sanctum is already a small monorepo for plugin-side work. No plumbing problem.
- The 14 implementation clicks under `c-moma9llq` are still a valid decomposition; they just stop being "commission units" and become "in-session work units" / progress markers.

## What changes vs. the handoff plan

**Drop:** brief drafting, commission posting, autonomous-agent context bundling, the framework-verification commission entirely as a *commission*.

**Keep:** the sequencing logic (foundation → schema → CLI → template → engines → archive → smoke → docs → port). The reasons it was good ordering for autonomous agents (unblock dependents first, parallel-safe in the middle) are even better when one operator is walking it.

**Recast:** "framework verification" becomes a direct source-code poke I do inline. I read the framework, write up findings, surface gaps to you. Output is the same; the wrapper around it disappears.

**Open design fork still real:** the archive-target question (c-momaa5o9 — lab books vs sanctum mirror vs hybrid). That's a conversation we have, not work I dispatch.

## Proposed first move

Two parallel threads, both me:

1. **Framework recon** — read the relevant framework source to answer: does `nsg plugin install` accept git SHAs? does the rig framework support shell/script engines (non-anima)? does Reckoner accept writ-type priority registration from kit contributions? what are the actual `nsg init` / `nsg codex add` surfaces? Write the findings as a short doc — `experiments/X???/...` is overkill, probably `.scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md` for now, then fold into the apparatus design.

2. **Archive-target conversation with you** — small fork in the road. I'll lay out the three options with trade-offs for you to pick between. Quick decision; unblocks the archive engine work.

Once both land, I walk the foundation children (plugin reshape → trial writ type / schema → manifest CLI → rig template → engines) in order, surfacing decisions as they arise.

## Question before I move

One thing the click hierarchy was sized for autonomous units — each child has enough scope to be a one-shot commission. With me walking them inline, do you want me to:

- **(a)** Keep the children as-is and use them as progress markers (tick through, conclude as I land each)
- **(b)** Collapse some — e.g., codex/guild fixture engines + scenario engines + probes are all "engines"; might be one work unit not three
- **(c)** Don't worry about it, ship work, conclude clicks pragmatically as natural break points appear

My instinct is **(c)** — the click decomposition was useful to *think* about scope; it doesn't need to map 1:1 to commits or work sessions now. But if you'd rather see clean conclude-per-child for traceability, I'll respect that.

Want me to kick off framework recon now, or pause for the archive design chat first?

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * The Fabricator — guild engine design registry apparatus.
3	 *
4	 * Scans installed engine designs from kit contributions and apparatus supportKits,
5	 * and serves them to the Spider on demand.
6	 *
7	 * The Fabricator does not execute engines. It is a pure query service:
8	 * designs in, designs out.
9	 */
10	
11	import type {
12	  StartupContext,
13	  Plugin,
14	} from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
15	
16	// ── Public types ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
17	
18	/** Minimal execution context passed to an engine's run() method. */
19	export interface EngineRunContext {
20	  /** The rig this engine instance belongs to. */
21	  rigId: string;
22	  /** Simple string identity for this engine instance (e.g. 'draft', 'implement'). */
23	  engineId: string;
24	  /** All upstream yields, keyed by engine id. Escape hatch for engines that need to inspect the full upstream chain. */
25	  upstream: Record<string, unknown>;
26	  /**
27	   * Present when this engine was previously blocked and has been restarted.
28	   * Advisory — do not depend on for correctness.
29	   *
30	   * Note: Defined inline to avoid a circular package dependency with spider-apparatus.
31	   * Shape matches spider-apparatus BlockRecord exactly.
32	   */
33	  priorBlock?: {
34	    type: string;
35	    condition: unknown;
36	    blockedAt: string;
37	    message?: string;
38	    lastCheckedAt?: string;
39	  };
40	}
41	
42	/**
43	 * The result of an engine run.
44	 *
45	 * 'completed' — synchronous work done inline, yields are available immediately.
46	 * 'launched'  — async work launched in a session; the Spider polls for completion.
47	 * 'blocked'   — engine is waiting for an external condition; Spider will poll
48	 *               the registered block type's checker and restart when cleared.
49	 */
50	export type EngineRunResult =
51	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
52	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
53	  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
54	
55	/**
56	 * Back-off growth parameters for an engine's retry policy. Matches
57	 * the shape Animator uses for its rate-limit back-off:
58	 *   - initialMs — first hold window in milliseconds
59	 *   - maxMs    — cap on the hold window in milliseconds
60	 *   - factor   — growth multiplier per consumed attempt
61	 */
62	export interface EngineRetryBackoffConfig {
63	  initialMs: number;
64	  maxMs: number;
65	  factor: number;
66	}
67	
68	/**
69	 * Opt-in retry policy for an engine design. When absent, the effective
70	 * policy is `maxAttempts: 0` — the engine fails terminally on the first
71	 * transient error with no retry. Explicit config enables retry.
72	 *
73	 * The Spider validates this shape at engine-design registration time
74	 * (see `validateEngineRetryConfig`); malformed values throw at startup.
75	 */
76	export interface EngineRetryConfig {
77	  /**
78	   * Total retry budget. `0` means fail-fast (no retry). `1` means one
79	   * retry (so up to two attempts total). Matches the "attempts consumed
80	   * from budget" semantics.
81	   */
82	  maxAttempts: number;
83	  /** Back-off growth parameters. Optional; defaults applied when omitted. */
84	  backoff?: Partial<EngineRetryBackoffConfig>;
85	}
86	
87	/**
88	 * An engine design — the unit of work the Fabricator catalogues and the
89	 * Spider executes. Kit authors import this type from @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus.
90	 */

content.content:
375	
376	/**
377	 * Clerk apparatus configuration — lives under the `clerk` key in guild.json.
378	 *
379	 * Writ types are now contributed exclusively via
380	 * `ClerkApi.registerWritType` from a plugin's own `start()`. There is no
381	 * guild-config `writTypes` field; the operator's only handle on the writ-
382	 * type registry from guild config is the `defaultType` selection (which is
383	 * validated against the registry once startup seals).
384	 */
385	export interface ClerkConfig {
386	  /** Default writ type when commission-post is called without a type (default: "mandate"). Validated against the writ-type registry at startup; an unregistered name fails fast. */
387	  defaultType?: string;
388	}
389	
390	// Augment GuildConfig so `guild().guildConfig().clerk` is typed without
391	// requiring a manual type parameter at the call site.
392	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
393	  interface GuildConfig {
394	    clerk?: ClerkConfig;
395	  }
396	}
397	
398	// ── Link documents ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
399	
400	/**
401	 * A link document as stored in The Stacks (clerk/links book).
402	 *
403	 * A writ link has two complementary identifiers for its relationship:
404	 *
405	 *   - `label` is a casual, human-facing string. It is open-ended, normalized
406	 *     at write time via the link-label normalization pipeline (lowercase,
407	 *     trim, camelCase split, snake_case/kebab-case split, whitespace
408	 *     collapse). Variant spellings of the same label collapse to a single
409	 *     canonical form; distinct labels remain distinct. Normalization is
410	 *     purely syntactic — it is NOT synonymy. `requires` and `depends on`
411	 *     normalize to different strings; callers that want to treat them as the
412	 *     same relationship should attach the same `kind` to both.
413	 *
414	 *   - `kind` is the load-bearing identifier. It is a stable, plugin-owned id
415	 *     drawn from the kit-contributed link-kind registry, and identifies the
416	 *     specific relationship a downstream consumer wants to react to
417	 *     (rendering parent/child, gating publish on `refines`, etc.). `null`
418	 *     when the caller treated this link as a casual label.
419	 */
420	export interface WritLinkDoc {
421	  /** Index signature required to satisfy BookEntry constraint. */
422	  [key: string]: unknown;
423	  /** Deterministic composite key: `{sourceId}:{targetId}:{normalized label}`. */
424	  id: string;
425	  /** The writ that is the origin of this relationship. */
426	  sourceId: string;
427	  /** The writ that is the target of this relationship. */
428	  targetId: string;
429	  /**
430	   * Casual relationship label — an open string normalized syntactically at
431	   * write time (e.g. "fixes", "retries", "supersedes", "duplicates",
432	   * "depends on"). NOT a synonymy layer. For load-bearing identity, use
433	   * `kind`.
434	   */
435	  label: string;
436	  /**
437	   * Load-bearing identifier for this relationship. When non-null, references
438	   * a registered kind id from the `linkKinds` kit registry and is the field
439	   * downstream consumers key on. Always present on each row (`null` when
440	   * the caller did not attach a kind).
441	   */
442	  kind: string | null;
443	  /** ISO timestamp when the link was created. */
444	  createdAt: string;
445	}
446	
447	// ── Link kind registry ───────────────────────────────────────────────
448	
449	/**
450	 * A link-kind contribution declared by a kit's `linkKinds` array.
451	 *
452	 * Kit authors use this shape when populating `ClerkKit.linkKinds`. Kind ids
453	 * must be prefixed with the contributing plugin's id followed by `.` and a
454	 * kebab-case suffix — e.g. kit `astrolabe` contributing `astrolabe.refines`.

1	/**
2	 * Plugin system — core types for the Kit/Apparatus model.
3	 *
4	 * Plugins come in two kinds:
5	 * - Kit:       passive package contributing capabilities to consuming apparatuses.
6	 *              No lifecycle, no running state. Read at load time.
7	 * - Apparatus: package contributing persistent running infrastructure.
8	 *              Has a start/stop lifecycle. Receives StartupContext at start.
9	 *
10	 * See: docs/architecture/plugins.md
11	 */
12	
13	// ── Loaded plugin descriptors ──────────────────────────────────────────
14	
15	/** A kit as tracked by the Arbor runtime. */
16	export interface LoadedKit {
17	  readonly packageName: string
18	  readonly id:          string
19	  readonly version:     string
20	  readonly kit:         Kit
21	}
22	
23	/** An apparatus as tracked by the Arbor runtime. */
24	export interface LoadedApparatus {
25	  readonly packageName: string
26	  readonly id:          string
27	  readonly version:     string
28	  readonly apparatus:   Apparatus
29	}
30	
31	/** Union of loaded kit and loaded apparatus. */
32	export type LoadedPlugin = LoadedKit | LoadedApparatus
33	
34	/** A plugin that failed to load, validate, or start. */
35	export interface FailedPlugin {
36	  readonly id:     string
37	  readonly reason: string
38	}
39	
40	// ── Context types ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
41	
42	// ── KitEntry ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
43	
44	/**
45	 * A single kit contribution collected during the Wire phase.
46	 *
47	 * Each key/value pair from a kit or supportKit (excluding framework fields
48	 * `requires` and `recommends`) becomes one KitEntry. Available via
49	 * `ctx.kits(type)` during and after `start()`.
50	 */
51	export interface KitEntry {
52	  /** Plugin id of the kit or apparatus that contributed this entry. */
53	  readonly pluginId: string
54	  /** npm package name of the contributing plugin. */
55	  readonly packageName: string
56	  /** The contribution key: 'tools', 'pages', 'routes', 'engines', 'roles', 'books', etc. */
57	  readonly type: string
58	  /** The contributed value — type varies by contribution type. */
59	  readonly value: unknown
60	}
61	
62	/**
63	 * Startup context passed to an apparatus's start(ctx).
64	 *
65	 * Provides lifecycle-event subscription and kit contribution queries.
66	 * All other guild access (apparatus APIs, config, home path, loaded plugins)
67	 * goes through the `guild()` singleton, which is available during start()
68	 * and in all handlers.
69	 *
70	 * See: docs/architecture/plugins.md
71	 */
72	export interface StartupContext {
73	  /** Subscribe to a guild lifecycle event. Handlers may be async; run sequentially. */
74	  on(event: string, handler: (...args: unknown[]) => void | Promise<void>): void
75	
76	  /**
77	   * Query kit contributions collected during the Wire phase.
78	   *
79	   * Returns all KitEntry records with the given contribution type.
80	   * Returns [] if no plugin contributes the requested type.
81	   * Each call returns a new array (snapshot copy).
82	   */
83	  kits(type: string): KitEntry[]
84	}
85	
86	// ── Kit ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
87	
88	/**
89	 * A kit — passive package contributing capabilities to consuming apparatuses.
90	 * Open record: contribution fields (engines, relays, tools, etc.) are defined
91	 * by the apparatus packages that consume them. `requires` and `recommends` are
92	 * the only framework-level fields.
93	 *
94	 * `requires`: apparatus names whose runtime APIs this kit's contributions depend
95	 *   on at handler invocation time. Hard startup validation failure if a declared
96	 *   apparatus is not installed.
97	 *
98	 * `recommends`: advisory apparatus names — generates startup warnings when
99	 *   expected apparatuses are absent. Not enforced.
100	 */
101	export type Kit = {
102	  requires?:   string[]
103	  recommends?: string[]
104	  [key: string]: unknown
105	}
106	
107	// ── Apparatus ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
108	
109	/**
110	 * An apparatus — package contributing persistent running infrastructure.
111	 * Has a start/stop lifecycle. Receives StartupContext at start.
112	 *
113	 * `requires`: apparatus names that must be started before this apparatus's
114	 *   start() runs. Determines start ordering. Hard startup validation failure
115	 *   if a declared apparatus is not installed.
116	 *
117	 * `recommends`: advisory apparatus names — generates startup warnings when
118	 *   expected apparatuses are absent. Not enforced — the apparatus starts
119	 *   regardless. Use for soft dependencies needed by optional API methods
120	 *   (e.g. The Animator recommends The Loom for summon(), but animate()
121	 *   works without it).
122	 *
123	 * `provides`: the runtime API object this apparatus exposes to other plugins.
124	 *   Retrieved via guild().apparatus<T>(name). Created at manifest-definition time,
125	 *   populated during start.
126	 *
127	 * `supportKit`: kit contributions this apparatus exposes to consuming apparatuses.
128	 *   Treated identically to standalone kit contributions by consumers.
129	 *
130	 * `consumes`: kit contribution field types this apparatus scans for and registers.
131	 *   Enables framework startup warnings when kits contribute types with no consumer.
132	 */
133	export type Apparatus = {
134	  requires?:    string[]
135	  recommends?:  string[]
136	  provides?:    unknown
137	  start:        (ctx: StartupContext) => void | Promise<void>
138	  stop?:        () => void | Promise<void>
139	  supportKit?:  Kit
140	  consumes?:    string[]
141	}
142	
143	// ── Plugin ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
144	
145	/**
146	 * The discriminated union plugin type. A plugin is either a kit or an apparatus.
147	 * The plugin name is always inferred from the npm package name at load time —
148	 * it is never declared in the manifest.
149	 */
150	export type Plugin =
151	  | { kit:       Kit }
152	  | { apparatus: Apparatus }
153	
154	// ── Type guards ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
155	
156	/** Type guard: is this value a kit plugin export? */
157	export function isKit(obj: unknown): obj is { kit: Kit } {
158	  return (
159	    typeof obj === 'object' &&
160	    obj !== null &&
161	    'kit' in obj &&
162	    typeof (obj as { kit: unknown }).kit === 'object' &&
163	    (obj as { kit: unknown }).kit !== null &&
164	    !Array.isArray((obj as { kit: unknown }).kit)
165	  )
166	}
167	
168	/** Type guard: is this value an apparatus plugin export? */
169	export function isApparatus(obj: unknown): obj is { apparatus: Apparatus } {
170	  return (
