toolUseResult.content:
# Laboratory apparatus — framework recon

Findings from reading the live framework source (the live `/workspace/nexus`
monorepo, on whatever SHA `nsg` is currently dispatching to). Maps each
prerequisite the apparatus design depends on to what the framework actually
offers, and surfaces gaps.

## TL;DR

All the load-bearing prerequisites are satisfied:

- **Non-anima engines: yes.** The Fabricator's `EngineDesign.run()` returns
  `{ status: 'completed', yields }` for synchronous work — exactly the shape
  we need for fixture/probe/scenario engines. Real precedent exists
  (`spider.seal` does git ops via Scriptorium and returns completed inline).
- **Plugin install accepts SHAs: yes** — `nsg plugin install` shells out to
  `npm install --save <source>`, which natively handles git+URL refs (and
  `#<sha>` pins on those URLs). The framework code passes the source string
  through unchanged.
- **Writ types via kit contributions: yes** — `ClerkApi.registerWritType`
  is called from the plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The Astrolabe plugin
  registers two custom types (`step`, `observation-set`) this way.
- **Rig templates via kit contributions: yes** — `SpiderKit.rigTemplates`
  + `SpiderKit.rigTemplateMappings` carry plugin-contributed templates and
  writ-type mappings.
- **Engine designs via kit contributions: yes** — `kit.engines: Record<id,
  EngineDesign>` is the standard shape; the Fabricator scans every kit's
  `engines` field and registers it under the contributing plugin's id.
- **Plugin CLI tools: yes** — tools registered via `supportKit.tools`
  auto-discover into `nsg <tool-name>` (with auto-grouping when names
  share a hyphen prefix). `nsg lab trial post` would come from a tool
  named `lab-trial-post`.
- **gh org permissions: yellow.** Org membership active; token scope is
  `repo`. Repo-create probably works (member-permitted in most org
  policies); repo-delete may not — admin perms needed depending on org
  policy. Need to verify by attempting once before relying on it.

The single piece the apparatus design got wrong is `nsg codex add` —
**it does NOT take a base SHA.** It only takes `(name, remoteUrl)`. The
SHA pinning happens earlier in the flow: the run-init script clones
upstream at the base SHA, pushes that as a fresh GH repo, then `nsg codex
add <new-repo>` clones the fresh repo at HEAD (which IS the base SHA by
construction). The original spec already describes this flow correctly;
the misread was about which step does the pinning.

No design adjustments required from the recon.

## Detailed findings

### 1. `nsg init`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts`

Surface: `nsg init <path> [--name <name>] [--model <model>]`

- Creates `<path>/`, `<path>/.nexus/`, `<path>/guild.json`,
  `<path>/package.json`, `<path>/.gitignore`.
- Refuses if `<path>` exists and is non-empty.
- Does NOT git init.
- Does NOT install plugins (separate step).
- Default model is `sonnet`.
- When framework is published (`VERSION !== '0.0.0'`), pins
  `@shardworks/nexus` in deps and runs `npm install`. In dev (link
  mode, VERSION = '0.0.0'), no install.

Gap vs. apparatus design: none. Spec already calls `nsg init <guild-dir>`
and follows up with separate `plugin install` / `codex add` steps.

### 2. `nsg plugin install`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts`

Surface: `nsg plugin install <source> [--type registry|link]`

Handler logic:
- If source is a path that exists and is a directory → link mode
  (`npm install --save file:<dir>` or `pnpm add link:<dir>`).
- Otherwise → registry mode (`npm install --save <source>`).

The `<source>` string is passed unchanged to npm. So:
- `@shardworks/foo@1.2.3` — semver works
- `@shardworks/foo@latest` — dist-tag works
- `git+https://github.com/foo/bar#<sha>` — SHA pin works (npm-native)
- `github:foo/bar#<sha>` — short-form git ref works
- `./local/path` — local link works

Plugin-id derivation: the framework parses the package name out of the
source string; for git URLs it falls back to reading the most recently
added entry in the guild's `package.json` after install (relies on
key insertion order — explicitly noted in code as a known compromise).

Gap vs. apparatus design: none. The handoff prompt's Q "does install
accept git SHAs" is yes, via standard npm git+URL syntax.

### 3. `nsg codex add`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/codexes/src/tools/codex-add.ts`

Surface: `nsg codex add --name <name> --remote-url <url>` (the auto-
grouping makes positional flags work too).

Handler logic: clones a bare copy to `.nexus/codexes/<name>.git`, registers
`{ remoteUrl }` under `codexes.registered[name]` in guild.json. **No SHA
parameter.** The clone is bare-default (HEAD is whatever the remote points
at).

Gap vs. apparatus design: the spec implied `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
<codex-base-sha>`; that flag does not exist. The fix is *order*, not
behavior: clone upstream at base SHA → push to fresh repo → `nsg codex add`
the fresh repo (which is at base SHA by construction). The spec text
"`gh repo create ... --source=<temp-dir> --push`" already describes this
correctly; only the example invocation `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
<codex-base-sha>` was misleading.

### 4. Engine designs (the load-bearing question)

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`

```ts
export interface EngineDesign {
  id: string;
  run(givens, context): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
  collect?(sessionId, givens, context): Promise<unknown>;
  retry?: EngineRetryConfig;
}

export type EngineRunResult =
  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
```

The `'completed'` branch is exactly what shell/script-style engines need.
No anima, no session, no Loom involvement — the engine does its work
synchronously in `run()` and returns yields directly. The Spider treats
the result as `engine-completed` and moves on.

Concrete precedent: `spider.seal` (in
`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts`) calls
`scriptorium.seal()` synchronously and returns
`{ status: 'completed', yields }`. Multiple Astrolabe engines do the
same (`plan-init`, `plan-finalize`, `inventory-check`, etc.).

Implication for the lab: every fixture, probe, and scenario engine I'll
write is a sync function that does shell/git/network/fs work and returns
yields. No new framework primitive needed.

Engines are registered via the plugin's `supportKit.engines: Record<id,
EngineDesign>`. The Fabricator scans every kit/supportKit's `engines`
field at startup. Two kits contributing the same id throws.

### 5. Writ-type registration

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`

`ClerkApi.registerWritType(config: WritTypeConfig)` is called from the
plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The registration window closes at
`phase:started`; calling after that throws.

Astrolabe registers `step` and `observation-set` this way. The Laboratory
will register `trial`. The `WritTypeConfig` shape lives in
`writ-type-config.ts` (state machine, child behavior, etc.).

### 6. Rig templates and mappings

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`

```ts
export interface SpiderKit {
  rigTemplates?: Record<string, RigTemplate>;
  rigTemplateMappings?: Record<string, string>;
}
```

Templates are keyed unqualified; the Spider registers them as
`<pluginId>.<key>`. Mappings map writ types to template names.

The Laboratory's `supportKit.rigTemplates` will carry
`'post-and-collect-default'`; mappings will route `trial` to
`'laboratory.post-and-collect-default'` (or whatever the resolved name is —
need to check whether mapping values use qualified or unqualified ids).

### 7. Plugin CLI tools

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/program.ts`

CLI surface for plugin-contributed tools: registered via the `tools`
apparatus (kit contribution `tools` field). The CLI auto-discovers and
auto-groups by hyphen prefix — `lab-trial-post`, `lab-trial-show`,
`lab-trial-list` would group under `nsg lab` as `trial-post`, etc. (need
to verify the exact grouping behavior — there's a `findGroupPrefixes`
function I didn't read yet).

### 8. gh permissions

Verified directly:
- Org membership in `shardworks`: active (role: member, direct).
- Token scopes: `gist`, `read:org`, `repo`. The `repo` scope is
  sufficient for `gh repo create` and `gh repo delete` IF the org policy
  permits.
- Permissions on `shardworks/nexus-mk2`: `{maintain: true, push: true,
  triage: true, pull: true, admin: false}`. Admin would simplify
  arbitrary repo mgmt; without it we depend on org-level repo policy.

What I haven't verified:
- Whether org members can create new repos in `shardworks` org. (Would
  need an org settings read or a test create.)
- Whether org members can delete repos they created. Default GitHub
  behavior is yes (creator can always delete), but it's worth confirming
  with a test once.

Recommendation: keep this as a yellow flag in the apparatus design.
Test it on the smoke run by attempting a real codex create + delete; if
it fails, surface to Sean and either bump token scopes / org perms or
adjust the apparatus to use a different repo namespace.

## What this changes about the apparatus design

Nothing fundamental. The design is implementable as written. Two minor
adjustments:

1. **`nsg codex add` invocation:** drop the imagined `--sha` flag. The
   codex-add step pins via the repo's HEAD (which is the base SHA after
   the gh repo create + push). The original spec text already describes
   this correctly; only the literal example invocation was wrong.

2. **Codex-fixture engine flow:** Make explicit that the engine must do
   the clone+push+create work BEFORE calling `nsg codex add`. Three
   substeps inside one engine, not three separate engines.

## Open questions for Sean

These are things the recon didn't decide for me; quick answers help shape
the implementation:

1. **Codex repo namespace.** Spec says "same GitHub org as the upstream
   codex" (`shardworks` for the dev case). Confirm that's the answer for
   v1, not e.g. a personal account or a dedicated `shardworks-experiments`
   org.

2. **Codex repo visibility.** Spec says `--private`. Confirm.

3. **Codex naming format.** Spec says
   `experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`. With trial as the
   primary writ type now (no separate experiment), names will be
   `trial-<trialId-or-slug>` or similar. Need to pick the actual format
   — and decide whether the "experiment" concept survives in the slug
   even though it doesn't have a writ type yet.

4. **Archive target (the still-open design question).** Three options:
   lab-host guild's books, sanctum mirror, or hybrid. Not blocked by
   recon — needs a separate conversation.

input.content:
# Laboratory apparatus — framework recon

Findings from reading the live framework source (the live `/workspace/nexus`
monorepo, on whatever SHA `nsg` is currently dispatching to). Maps each
prerequisite the apparatus design depends on to what the framework actually
offers, and surfaces gaps.

## TL;DR

All the load-bearing prerequisites are satisfied:

- **Non-anima engines: yes.** The Fabricator's `EngineDesign.run()` returns
  `{ status: 'completed', yields }` for synchronous work — exactly the shape
  we need for fixture/probe/scenario engines. Real precedent exists
  (`spider.seal` does git ops via Scriptorium and returns completed inline).
- **Plugin install accepts SHAs: yes** — `nsg plugin install` shells out to
  `npm install --save <source>`, which natively handles git+URL refs (and
  `#<sha>` pins on those URLs). The framework code passes the source string
  through unchanged.
- **Writ types via kit contributions: yes** — `ClerkApi.registerWritType`
  is called from the plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The Astrolabe plugin
  registers two custom types (`step`, `observation-set`) this way.
- **Rig templates via kit contributions: yes** — `SpiderKit.rigTemplates`
  + `SpiderKit.rigTemplateMappings` carry plugin-contributed templates and
  writ-type mappings.
- **Engine designs via kit contributions: yes** — `kit.engines: Record<id,
  EngineDesign>` is the standard shape; the Fabricator scans every kit's
  `engines` field and registers it under the contributing plugin's id.
- **Plugin CLI tools: yes** — tools registered via `supportKit.tools`
  auto-discover into `nsg <tool-name>` (with auto-grouping when names
  share a hyphen prefix). `nsg lab trial post` would come from a tool
  named `lab-trial-post`.
- **gh org permissions: yellow.** Org membership active; token scope is
  `repo`. Repo-create probably works (member-permitted in most org
  policies); repo-delete may not — admin perms needed depending on org
  policy. Need to verify by attempting once before relying on it.

The single piece the apparatus design got wrong is `nsg codex add` —
**it does NOT take a base SHA.** It only takes `(name, remoteUrl)`. The
SHA pinning happens earlier in the flow: the run-init script clones
upstream at the base SHA, pushes that as a fresh GH repo, then `nsg codex
add <new-repo>` clones the fresh repo at HEAD (which IS the base SHA by
construction). The original spec already describes this flow correctly;
the misread was about which step does the pinning.

No design adjustments required from the recon.

## Detailed findings

### 1. `nsg init`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts`

Surface: `nsg init <path> [--name <name>] [--model <model>]`

- Creates `<path>/`, `<path>/.nexus/`, `<path>/guild.json`,
  `<path>/package.json`, `<path>/.gitignore`.
- Refuses if `<path>` exists and is non-empty.
- Does NOT git init.
- Does NOT install plugins (separate step).
- Default model is `sonnet`.
- When framework is published (`VERSION !== '0.0.0'`), pins
  `@shardworks/nexus` in deps and runs `npm install`. In dev (link
  mode, VERSION = '0.0.0'), no install.

Gap vs. apparatus design: none. Spec already calls `nsg init <guild-dir>`
and follows up with separate `plugin install` / `codex add` steps.

### 2. `nsg plugin install`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts`

Surface: `nsg plugin install <source> [--type registry|link]`

Handler logic:
- If source is a path that exists and is a directory → link mode
  (`npm install --save file:<dir>` or `pnpm add link:<dir>`).
- Otherwise → registry mode (`npm install --save <source>`).

The `<source>` string is passed unchanged to npm. So:
- `@shardworks/foo@1.2.3` — semver works
- `@shardworks/foo@latest` — dist-tag works
- `git+https://github.com/foo/bar#<sha>` — SHA pin works (npm-native)
- `github:foo/bar#<sha>` — short-form git ref works
- `./local/path` — local link works

Plugin-id derivation: the framework parses the package name out of the
source string; for git URLs it falls back to reading the most recently
added entry in the guild's `package.json` after install (relies on
key insertion order — explicitly noted in code as a known compromise).

Gap vs. apparatus design: none. The handoff prompt's Q "does install
accept git SHAs" is yes, via standard npm git+URL syntax.

### 3. `nsg codex add`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/codexes/src/tools/codex-add.ts`

Surface: `nsg codex add --name <name> --remote-url <url>` (the auto-
grouping makes positional flags work too).

Handler logic: clones a bare copy to `.nexus/codexes/<name>.git`, registers
`{ remoteUrl }` under `codexes.registered[name]` in guild.json. **No SHA
parameter.** The clone is bare-default (HEAD is whatever the remote points
at).

Gap vs. apparatus design: the spec implied `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
<codex-base-sha>`; that flag does not exist. The fix is *order*, not
behavior: clone upstream at base SHA → push to fresh repo → `nsg codex add`
the fresh repo (which is at base SHA by construction). The spec text
"`gh repo create ... --source=<temp-dir> --push`" already describes this
correctly; only the example invocation `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
<codex-base-sha>` was misleading.

### 4. Engine designs (the load-bearing question)

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`

```ts
export interface EngineDesign {
  id: string;
  run(givens, context): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
  collect?(sessionId, givens, context): Promise<unknown>;
  retry?: EngineRetryConfig;
}

export type EngineRunResult =
  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
```

The `'completed'` branch is exactly what shell/script-style engines need.
No anima, no session, no Loom involvement — the engine does its work
synchronously in `run()` and returns yields directly. The Spider treats
the result as `engine-completed` and moves on.

Concrete precedent: `spider.seal` (in
`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts`) calls
`scriptorium.seal()` synchronously and returns
`{ status: 'completed', yields }`. Multiple Astrolabe engines do the
same (`plan-init`, `plan-finalize`, `inventory-check`, etc.).

Implication for the lab: every fixture, probe, and scenario engine I'll
write is a sync function that does shell/git/network/fs work and returns
yields. No new framework primitive needed.

Engines are registered via the plugin's `supportKit.engines: Record<id,
EngineDesign>`. The Fabricator scans every kit/supportKit's `engines`
field at startup. Two kits contributing the same id throws.

### 5. Writ-type registration

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`

`ClerkApi.registerWritType(config: WritTypeConfig)` is called from the
plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The registration window closes at
`phase:started`; calling after that throws.

Astrolabe registers `step` and `observation-set` this way. The Laboratory
will register `trial`. The `WritTypeConfig` shape lives in
`writ-type-config.ts` (state machine, child behavior, etc.).

### 6. Rig templates and mappings

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`

```ts
export interface SpiderKit {
  rigTemplates?: Record<string, RigTemplate>;
  rigTemplateMappings?: Record<string, string>;
}
```

Templates are keyed unqualified; the Spider registers them as
`<pluginId>.<key>`. Mappings map writ types to template names.

The Laboratory's `supportKit.rigTemplates` will carry
`'post-and-collect-default'`; mappings will route `trial` to
`'laboratory.post-and-collect-default'` (or whatever the resolved name is —
need to check whether mapping values use qualified or unqualified ids).

### 7. Plugin CLI tools

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/program.ts`

CLI surface for plugin-contributed tools: registered via the `tools`
apparatus (kit contribution `tools` field). The CLI auto-discovers and
auto-groups by hyphen prefix — `lab-trial-post`, `lab-trial-show`,
`lab-trial-list` would group under `nsg lab` as `trial-post`, etc. (need
to verify the exact grouping behavior — there's a `findGroupPrefixes`
function I didn't read yet).

### 8. gh permissions

Verified directly:
- Org membership in `shardworks`: active (role: member, direct).
- Token scopes: `gist`, `read:org`, `repo`. The `repo` scope is
  sufficient for `gh repo create` and `gh repo delete` IF the org policy
  permits.
- Permissions on `shardworks/nexus-mk2`: `{maintain: true, push: true,
  triage: true, pull: true, admin: false}`. Admin would simplify
  arbitrary repo mgmt; without it we depend on org-level repo policy.

What I haven't verified:
- Whether org members can create new repos in `shardworks` org. (Would
  need an org settings read or a test create.)
- Whether org members can delete repos they created. Default GitHub
  behavior is yes (creator can always delete), but it's worth confirming
  with a test once.

Recommendation: keep this as a yellow flag in the apparatus design.
Test it on the smoke run by attempting a real codex create + delete; if
it fails, surface to Sean and either bump token scopes / org perms or
adjust the apparatus to use a different repo namespace.

## What this changes about the apparatus design

Nothing fundamental. The design is implementable as written. Two minor
adjustments:

1. **`nsg codex add` invocation:** drop the imagined `--sha` flag. The
   codex-add step pins via the repo's HEAD (which is the base SHA after
   the gh repo create + push). The original spec text already describes
   this correctly; only the literal example invocation was wrong.

2. **Codex-fixture engine flow:** Make explicit that the engine must do
   the clone+push+create work BEFORE calling `nsg codex add`. Three
   substeps inside one engine, not three separate engines.

## Open questions for Sean

These are things the recon didn't decide for me; quick answers help shape
the implementation:

1. **Codex repo namespace.** Spec says "same GitHub org as the upstream
   codex" (`shardworks` for the dev case). Confirm that's the answer for
   v1, not e.g. a personal account or a dedicated `shardworks-experiments`
   org.

2. **Codex repo visibility.** Spec says `--private`. Confirm.

3. **Codex naming format.** Spec says
   `experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`. With trial as the
   primary writ type now (no separate experiment), names will be
   `trial-<trialId-or-slug>` or similar. Need to pick the actual format
   — and decide whether the "experiment" concept survives in the slug
   even though it doesn't have a writ type yet.

4. **Archive target (the still-open design question).** Three options:
   lab-host guild's books, sanctum mirror, or hybrid. Not blocked by
   recon — needs a separate conversation.

content.text:
Status update — recon done. Findings doc is at `.scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md`. Key takeaways:

## Headline

**Everything load-bearing checks out.** Non-anima engines are first-class (`{ status: 'completed', yields }`), with real precedent (`spider.seal` is exactly this pattern). Plugin install accepts SHAs via standard npm git+URL syntax. Writ-type, rig-template, and engine-design contributions all flow through the kit-contribution mechanism. The Astrolabe plugin is a clean reference for what a multi-contribution apparatus looks like.

## One spec correction

The handoff spec implied `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha <base-sha>` — that flag doesn't exist. `codex add` only takes `(name, remoteUrl)`. The spec's *flow* is right (clone upstream at base SHA → push to fresh repo → add the fresh repo), it's just the imagined invocation that was wrong. SHA pinning happens in the repo itself, not in `codex add`. Tiny: the codex-fixture engine becomes a 3-step thing internally, not 3 engines.

## One yellow flag

`gh` token has `repo` scope and I'm an active member of `shardworks`, but **`admin` is false on the nexus-mk2 repo**. Org-level repo create/delete permission for members depends on org policy I haven't probed. Reasonable bet it works; worth verifying on the first smoke run rather than late in development.

## Things only you can answer

1. **Codex repo home** — spec says "same org as upstream codex" (so `shardworks`). Confirm, or do you want `shardworks-experiments` / a personal namespace / other?
2. **Repo visibility** — spec says `--private`. Confirm?
3. **Codex naming format** — spec said `experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`, but that was when "experiment" was a writ type. With trial as the primary writ type now, what's the slug shape? Some options:
   - `trial-<trialId>` (uses the writ id; ugly but unambiguous)
   - `trial-<human-slug-from-manifest>` (manifest carries a slug field; readable)
   - Keep `experiment-…-run-…` informally even though "experiment" isn't a writ (people still group trials into experiments mentally)
4. **Archive target** — still open from the click tree (`c-momaa5o9`). Three options to discuss when you're ready: lab-host guild's books, sanctum mirror, or hybrid.

## Suggested next step

Foundation work is unblocked. I can dive into **plugin reshape** (gut the no-op stub, set up the apparatus skeleton with `requires`, `supportKit`, `start`, the writ-type registration call, etc.) without waiting for answers to the codex/archive questions — the scaffolding doesn't depend on them. Codex naming and archive design can land before I write the codex-fixture and archive engines specifically.

Want me to start the plugin reshape, or pause here for the design conversation first?

input.content:
# The Laboratory

Apparatus for running trial-shaped experiments on guild configurations.

## Audiences

- **Nexus dev** — cost/quality tuning, prompt evaluation, plugin variant
  comparison. Replaces the standalone-bash spec at
  `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md`.
- **End users** — evaluate prompts, plugins, and config variants by
  authoring trial manifests against a stable apparatus surface.

## Architecture (MVP0)

- **Writ type:** `trial` — a single execution unit. Lifecycle mirrors
  mandate (`new → open → completed | failed | cancelled`, with `stuck`
  as a non-terminal off `open`). Trials are leaves in v1; the
  higher-level `experiment` grouping is parked for v2.
- **Rig template:** `post-and-collect-default` — composes
  fixture-setup, scenario, probe, teardown, and archive engines from
  the writ's `ext.laboratory.config`. One canonical template; extension
  is via plugin contributions, not in-template slots.
- **Engines:**
  - **Fixtures** — set up and tear down disposable surfaces (codex
    repos, test guilds). Form a dep DAG; topo-sorted at template
    instantiation.
  - **Scenario** — the workload. v1 uses cross-guild commission-post +
    wait-for-writ-terminal as the canonical scenario engine pair.
  - **Probes** — extract data from one or more fixtures (stacks dump,
    git range capture).
  - **Archive** — captures probe outputs for the research record.
    Storage layout in design at click `c-momaa5o9`.
- **Authoring:** YAML manifest via `nsg lab trial post --manifest <file>`.
  Manifest shape mirrors `ext.laboratory.config` exactly.

## Status

Skeleton only. The trial writ type is registered; engine designs, the
rig template, and the manifest CLI are added by subsequent
implementation children under click `c-moma9llq`.

## Background

This package previously held a CDC-based observational stub (data
mirroring of writs/sessions into the sanctum). That instrument was
retired 2026-04-30 — its underlying signals (patron-set spec quality
ratings, structured commission review) had been hollowed out by the
shift to automated planning and static review pipelines. The package
was kept as a no-op so existing `guild.json` registrations stayed
loadable; the apparatus reshape reuses the package and the registered
plugin id, but the old data-mirroring code and types are gone.

lines: -Retired 2026-04-30. The Laboratory was an observational apparatus that watched Stacks CDC events on the Clerk's writs and links books and the Animator's sessions book, mirroring observational data into the sanctum at `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml` and `experiments/data/commissions/<id>/`. Its purpose was to feed the X013 (Commission Outcomes) research instrument with patron-subjective judgments alongside auto-collected telemetry.
+Apparatus for running trial-shaped experiments on guild configurations.
-Two pieces of the surrounding system shifted in ways that hollowed out the instrument:
+- **Nexus dev** — cost/quality tuning, prompt evaluation, plugin variant
+  comparison. Replaces the standalone-bash spec at
+- **End users** — evaluate prompts, plugins, and config variants by
+  authoring trial manifests against a stable apparatus surface.

-- **Spec generation became automated.** The Astrolabe planning pipeline now produces every commission's spec from a brief. Spec quality no longer varies along a patron-craft axis, so `spec_quality_pre/post` ratings are constant by construction.

-- **Structured patron review was retired.** The patron stopped routinely filling in `complexity`, `outcome`, `failure_mode`, and `reviewed_at`. Per-commission review became ad-hoc as the planning workshop and static implement→review→revise rig pipeline absorbed the quality-assurance role.

-Without those signals, what remained in the commission log and per-commission directories was strictly mechanical — auto-set outcome (success/abandoned), the writ body as `commission.md`, and session telemetry — all of which is fully reproducible from the guild's own books.
+- **Writ type:** `trial` — a single execution unit. Lifecycle mirrors
+  mandate (`new → open → completed | failed | cancelled`, with `stuck`
+  as a non-terminal off `open`). Trials are leaves in v1; the

+  - **Archive** — captures probe outputs for the research record.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# The Laboratory (retired)

Retired 2026-04-30. The Laboratory was an observational apparatus that watched Stacks CDC events on the Clerk's writs and links books and the Animator's sessions book, mirroring observational data into the sanctum at `experiments/data/commission-log.yaml` and `experiments/data/commissions/<id>/`. Its purpose was to feed the X013 (Commission Outcomes) research instrument with patron-subjective judgments alongside auto-collected telemetry.

## Why it was retired

Two pieces of the surrounding system shifted in ways that hollowed out the instrument:

- **Spec generation became automated.** The Astrolabe planning pipeline now produces every commission's spec from a brief. Spec quality no longer varies along a patron-craft axis, so `spec_quality_pre/post` ratings are constant by construction.
- **Structured patron review was retired.** The patron stopped routinely filling in `complexity`, `outcome`, `failure_mode`, and `reviewed_at`. Per-commission review became ad-hoc as the planning workshop and static implement→review→revise rig pipeline absorbed the quality-assurance role.

Without those signals, what remained in the commission log and per-commission directories was strictly mechanical — auto-set outcome (success/abandoned), the writ body as `commission.md`, and session telemetry — all of which is fully reproducible from the guild's own books.

X013 was moved to Superseded; the Laboratory's three CDC watchers and helper code were deleted; this package was reduced to a no-op stub so existing guild.json registrations stay loadable.

## Where the data lives now

| Signal | Source |
|---|---|
| Writ status and lifecycle | `clerk/writs` book — query via `nsg writ show` / `nsg writ list` / `nsg writ tree` |
| Writ relationships (`fixes`, `depends-on`, etc.) | `clerk/links` book — query via `nsg writ` link tools |
| Session telemetry (cost, tokens, duration, exit, output) | `animator/sessions` book — query via `nsg session show` / `nsg session list` |
| Anima role and engine id per session | `animator/sessions` book — `metadata.role`, `metadata.engineId` |
| Engine→session linkage within a rig | `spider/rigs` book — query via `nsg rig for-writ` / `nsg rig show` |

## Historical baseline

The pruned commission log (150 patron-touched entries spanning 2026-03-25 → 2026-04-29) is preserved as an artifact in the experiments that reference it:

- `experiments/X013-commission-outcomes/artifacts/2026-04-30-commission-log-frozen-baseline.yaml` — owned by the experiment that produced it.
- `experiments/X008-patrons-hands/artifacts/2026-04-30-commission-log-frozen-baseline.yaml` — referenced by §Infrastructure Milestones for the H5 review-rate-cliff evidence.

The 22 commission directories (out of 1246) that contained substantive patron-written `review.md` notes remain at `experiments/data/commissions/<id>/`. The other 1224 — pure auto-generated content — were deleted.

## Patron-side cleanup (non-urgent)

The plugin is currently a no-op. Once you are ready to fully retire the package:

1. Remove the `"laboratory"` entry from `/workspace/vibers/guild.json` plugins list.
2. Remove `@shardworks/laboratory-apparatus` from `/workspace/vibers/package.json` dependencies.
3. Restart the guild.
4. Delete `packages/laboratory/` from the sanctum.

lastPrompt: 1 - confirm same org; 2 - sure, let's do private just to be tidy; 3 - lets do `trial-<slug-with-length-limit>-<trialId-prefix>`; go ahead and start

content.content:
date: 2026-04-30
  item: "Build h5_implementation_tail.py (X011 instrument) to test whether implementer sessions have a natural phase boundary for the P3 idea #18 (implement-core + implement-finalize 2-split). Walks 84 implementer-shaped sessions filtered from 659 nexus-draft jsonls; classifies each turn by tool-call signature (EDIT/VERIFY/READ/MIXED/OTHER); computes last-edit position, tail length, decile edit-density, decline-from-peak decile, and tail composition. Findings: no phase boundary exists. Tests are interleaved throughout (median 8 verify Bashes per session, not batched at end). Post-last-edit tail exists at median 12% of session but is 81% bookkeeping (git/ls/cleanup), only 13% verify — there's nothing for a finalize session to do. Edit-density bell-curves through deciles 5-7 with gradual decline. Wrote findings artifact 2026-04-30-implementation-tail-analysis.md. Bug encountered and fixed: BASH_MODIFY regex in the existing h4_read_utilization.py pattern was matching '2>&1' as a file-redirection (because '\b...\b|>\s*\S+|...' had '>&1' satisfying the pattern), suppressing all in-session verify Bash detection; tightened to require the redirect target to look like a file path. Concluded c-mok4qhn9 (idea #18, refuted) and amended c-mok4rfuk (P3) goal to make cost-midpoint split the canonical interpretation, not phase boundary."
  commissionable: true

- session: 8e0046d8-dc49-4e75-b8c8-79f33ea5115b
  date: 2026-04-30
  item: "Decommission the commission-log instrument and the Laboratory plugin's CDC collection. Patron determination: spec generation has become automated through the Astrolabe pipeline (eliminating spec-quality variance) and structured patron review has been retired (no new outcome / failure_mode / reviewed_at capture going forward), so the commission-log's manual fields and the file-based session/folder mirroring no longer earn their keep — all signal is reproducible from the writs+links+animator-sessions+spider-rigs books. Three commits across the cluster: (1) data prune — commission-log.yaml 1244→150 entries (kept only patron-touched: custom complexity, custom outcomes (partial/wrong), notes, reviewed_at, reviewed_by_ethnographer); commissions/ 1246→22 folders (kept those with substantive patron review.md, deleted auto-only). (2) code retire — gutted packages/laboratory/src/ (deleted laboratory.ts, yaml-writer.ts, quality-trigger.ts, git.ts and their tests; reduced index.ts to a no-op apparatus + types.ts to GuildConfig augmentation only); deleted bin/commission.sh after determining it was a thin wrapper providing only first-line title extraction + @file body shorthand; refactored bin/plan-review.ts to derive titles in JS and spawn nsg commission-post directly. (3) docs sweep — coco.md startup section drops the commission-log sweep and Commission Review Tracking; ethnographer.md drops the commission-probing protocol; .claude/CLAUDE.md project-structure tree drops the commission.sh entry; briefs skill rewrites posting examples to call nsg commission-post directly. X013 moved Active→Superseded with a multi-line frontmatter superseded_reason; X008 §Infrastructure Milestones gains a 2026-04-30 note explaining that reviewed_at instrumentation is retired (future H5 evidence is qualitative; the retirement itself is consistent with H5's prediction of system-internalized criteria displacing per-commission review). Created and concluded 9 subclicks under c-mof68n1v plus an additional one for the script removal; concluded the parent click. Outstanding: remove 'laboratory' from /workspace/vibers/guild.json plugins list + the @shardworks/laboratory-apparatus link in /workspace/vibers/package.json once Sean restarts the guild (non-urgent — plugin is now a no-op)."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 8e0046d8-dc49-4e75-b8c8-79f33ea5115b
  date: 2026-04-30
  item: "Distribute the frozen commission-log baseline into the experiments that consume it, then remove the shared file. Sean's logic: only X008 actively consults the baseline (for §Infrastructure Milestones H5 review-rate-cliff evidence), and only X013 owns it (the experiment that produced the data). Copied commission-log.yaml into both experiments' artifacts/ as 2026-04-30-commission-log-frozen-baseline.yaml; rewrote each copy's header with experiment-specific framing (X013 version says 'frozen because superseded'; X008 version explains the H5 evidence and points back at §Infrastructure Milestones). Updated the X008 spec note's link to point at the local artifact. Updated the X013 spec frontmatter superseded_reason and added a redirect banner at the top of §Data Collection so the historical narrative below (which describes the live instrument) reads as bracketed historical content. Updated the experiments/index.md X013 supersession entry. Rewrote packages/laboratory/README.md from end to end — was 150 lines describing the live CDC behavior, now ~50 lines explaining retirement, where the data lives now, and patron-side cleanup steps. Rewrote experiments/data/commissions/README.md similarly — was the two-tier-architecture description, now explains the directory is frozen and points at where new data lives. Updated packages/laboratory/src/index.ts retirement comment to refer to 'a sanctum filesystem tree' rather than the specific commission-log path (which no longer exists). Deleted experiments/data/commission-log.yaml. The artifact copies in X008 and X013 are now the canonical historical reference; the shared file is gone."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 8e0046d8-dc49-4e75-b8c8-79f33ea5115b
  date: 2026-04-30
  item: "Draft X013 retrospective at experiments/X013-commission-outcomes/artifacts/2026-04-30-retrospective.md. Pulled cross-tabs from the frozen baseline to extract directional evidence per hypothesis: H1 (spec_quality × outcome) shows 75% success on 'strong' specs (n=59), 53% on 'adequate' (n=17), 0% on 'weak' (n=2 — too small) — gradient in the predicted direction but well below significance; H2 (complexity × revision_required) shows 33% / 50% / 57% revision rates across small / medium / large buckets (n=34 total, way below the 30-per-bucket the spec called for) — also directional, also dose-response rather than threshold-shaped; H3 (reviewed_at distribution over time) shows the strongest signal — 17 reviews on Apr-2, 6 on Apr-3, 2 on Apr-4 (the cliff), then ad-hoc trickle to Apr-29 (40 reviews total) — caused by the static review rig + planning workshop landings, lifted into X008 H5 going forward; H4 (attribution) untested, but failure_mode taxonomy was used differentially (execution_error/incomplete/requirement_wrong/broken/spec_ambiguous all populated). Captured methodological observations: pre/post separation worked structurally; complexity rating decayed faster than spec_quality; the W3 hardcoded engine.id finding as the cleanest H1 anecdote; the cliff shape was the surprise (step function, not gradual maturation). Mapped what successors inherit (X008 already has the cliff signal; X014 needs design rethink without patron-authored arm; X015 is the direct H1 successor in the spec-automation era; spec scorer anima / writ-graph revision rate / assess-writ CLI futures are now obsolete in their original framing). Updated X013 spec.md frontmatter superseded_reason with a pointer to the retro."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum


- session: 8dd39ccd-28cc-45eb-8cdb-447d0169b353
  date: 2026-04-30
  item: "Draft experimental-infrastructure spec for P3 empirical trials. Walks the design through three-repo separation (sanctum as canonical record / nexus experiment branches / short-lived codex forks per trial), one-guild-per-trial model, manifest-driven setup, file-per-table JSON stacks dump format, per-commit codex-history capture as diffs. Captures init/archive/cleanup script CLIs, artifact-dir layout (trial-NNN-<variant>/ with prompts/transcripts/stacks-export/codex-history/laboratory/analysis subdirs), trial README format, naming conventions, trial-vs-experiment discipline (one X### per research question). Includes worked example for hypothetical X016-p3-orientation-suppression and a tail of 8 open framework-mapping questions (nsg init shape, plugin pinning, etc.) that need answering before scripts can be built. Spec at experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md. Created click c-molveg89 under c-modxxtu6 (P3 idea #15) tracking the spec as a prerequisite for empirical Q4-Q8 testing. Wrote handoff prompt at .scratch/handoff-experiment-infrastructure-spec.md for Sean to use in a fresh session for spec refinement."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 0ffe03af-0a2c-4feb-80f0-5d9071d2227a
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Refine experimental-infrastructure spec based on Sean's annotations. Vocabulary swap: 'trial' -> 'run' throughout (file/dir names, scripts, prose); the run framing strips off scientific-trial baggage we don't earn (we're capturing executions of an apparatus with controlled+manipulated variables, not running comparison-arm hypothesis tests). Manifest split: run.yaml (immutable inputs) + run-status.yaml (mutable lifecycle), with status keyed by plugin ID per Nexus convention -- everything sits under laboratory.{phase, setup, archive}; phase defaults to 'new'; archive overwrites in place with no preservation of prior runs. Manifest restructure: guild.{plugins, config, files} umbrella subsumes prompts_dir; plugins[].version (not ref) accepts SHAs/semver/local paths; archive_extras dropped. Setup script switches from bespoke guild.json/package.json generation to standard guild commands (nsg init -> nsg plugin install -> nsg codex add -> merge guild.config -> copy guild.files). Codex repo is now a fresh repo via gh repo create + push (not a GitHub fork) to avoid leaving fork metadata on upstream. Per-run nexus branching with no enforced cardinality (each run declares its own branch+SHA; runs sharing state share a branch). Laboratory regains relevance with two new tools: nsg lab export-books (dumps every book to JSON-per-table) and nsg lab git-range (generic commit-range diff dump, useful beyond this infra); promotion to stacks plugin if proven. Transcripts now come from the animator book (no ~/.claude/projects dependency, sidesteps multi-provider question). Codex-history commits-manifest drops the seq field (YAML order suffices) and per-commit cross-references (live in books export). Experiment discipline replaces trial discipline -- relaxed from 'one experiment = one hypothesis' to 'one experiment = one primary research question; secondary findings allowed; no HARKing'; explained the shift in chat (real science doesn't enforce one-hypothesis partition; what it disciplines is preregistration, multiple-comparisons correction, no result-fitting). Trimmed 8 open implementation questions down to 6 concrete prerequisites (nsg command-surface verification + Laboratory tool builds). Worked example updated to use runs throughout."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 905f54f8-fd5c-49df-9294-b856202a7740
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Design Laboratory MVP0 apparatus from rabbit-hole exploration to commissionable plan. Started from cost-reduction motivation that broadened into a generalized experimentation toolkit pitch. Captured in click tree under c-mom18vmh: eight foundational design decisions concluded (writ-shape splits experiment from trial; writ name = 'trial'; data home = lab-host guild; keep 'Laboratory' name; fixture/probe split; trial-only fixture scope with v2 hooks; scenario IS a rig template; ext.laboratory.config + YAML manifest authoring). Implementation companion c-moma9llq with 14 commissionable children (framework prereq verification, plugin reshape, trial writ type + ext schema, manifest CLI, post-and-collect-default rig template, codex/guild fixture engines, cross-guild scenario engines, probe engines, archive engine + design, smoke test, retire infra spec, docs, port first real trial). Two parked: cross-guild engine surface (c-mom9vm3n) and v2 experiment-as-grouping concept (c-momaacry). Key reframings during the conversation: pushed back on Sean's initial 'experiment as writ type' framing — split into experiment (research unit) and trial (writ-shaped execution unit); Sean's reasoning in turn collapsed the proposed scenario primitive into 'scenario IS a rig template' (cancels new substrate — reuses Spider/Reckoner/engines). Decisions deferred: archive output target (lab books vs sanctum vs hybrid + storage layout + writ→archive linkage; gates archive impl); framework verification commission to dispatch first (does nsg plugin install accept SHAs? does the rig framework support non-anima/script engines? — load-bearing). No file changes other than this session note and coco-log update; design captured entirely in click tree."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

content.text:
Committed. Status update:

## Where we are

**Two clicks down**, four lines of work freshly unblocked.

✓ **Framework recon** — done; everything load-bearing checks out.
✓ **Plugin reshape** — apparatus skeleton landed; trial writ type registered; types.ts has the provisional `LaboratoryTrialConfig` schema; orphan tests purged; builds clean.

## What's next

Next up the planned sequencing is the **trial writ type + ext schema** child (`c-moma9sg5`), but that's mostly already done — I declared `TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG` and the `LaboratoryTrialConfig` shape in this commit. Two real choices on what to do next:

**Option A: Manifest CLI (`c-moma9ty6`)** — author the YAML manifest format, the parser/validator, and the `nsg lab trial post` command. This makes the apparatus *authorable* even before any engines exist; a user could post a trial and watch it sit at `new` because there's no rig template wired yet. Useful for end-to-end skeleton testing.

**Option B: Rig template + skeleton engines (`c-moma9vrm`)** — author the `post-and-collect-default` template and stub-implement each engine type as a "logs and yields nothing" placeholder. This makes the apparatus *executable* before authorable: trials posted via raw `nsg commission-post` would crawl through the template and complete trivially. Useful for verifying the rig wiring before real engine logic.

**Option C: Engine-first (any of `c-moma9y1k`, `c-momaa03d`, `c-momaa1vt`, `c-momaa3w7`)** — pick the most-load-bearing engine (probably `lab.guild-setup` since that's the gnarliest in terms of framework integration: `nsg init` + `nsg plugin install` per pin + `nsg codex add` + deep-merge + file copy) and ship it. Defer the template and CLI until we have at least one real engine to wire.

## My recommendation

**Option B (rig template + skeleton engines)** for one reason: the rig template is where the architectural risk lives. If the spec's mental model of "topo-sort fixtures → run scenario → run probes → archive" doesn't translate cleanly into a flat `RigTemplate.engines[]` array, we want to find out before we've invested in real engine implementations or a CLI. Stubbed engines that just log + yield are cheap; they validate the wiring without any real work.

CLI and engines come *after* we know the template shape works.

## Other thing I noticed

The sanctum's root `package.json` only links `@shardworks/nexus-core` and `@shardworks/stacks-apparatus` from the live `/workspace/nexus` monorepo via `pnpm.overrides`. The other apparatuses (clerk, codexes, fabricator, spider) get pulled from npm at version 0.1.292. That's fine for typechecking against a stable API, but if you ever push framework changes and want them reflected here without bumping npm, we'd want to add the extra link overrides. Vibers does this consistently. Not blocking — just something to flag.

Continue with Option B?
Got it — overrides first (quick), then option B. Let me also peek at how dynamic per-trial composition can work since the trial flow is config-dependent.

content.content:
300	 * A single engine slot declared in a rig template.
301	 */
302	export interface RigTemplateEngine {
303	  /** Engine id unique within this template. */
304	  id: string;
305	  /** Engine design id to look up in the Fabricator. */
306	  designId: string;
307	  /** Engine ids within this template whose completion is required first. Defaults to []. */
308	  upstream?: string[];
309	  /**
310	   * Givens to pass to the engine.
311	   *
312	   * String values may contain `${...}` template expressions:
313	   *   `${writ}` — the full WritDoc for this rig's writ
314	   *   `${writ.<path>}` — a field of the WritDoc (dot-path traversal)
315	   *   `${vars.<path>}` — value from spider.variables config (dot-path traversal)
316	   *   `${yields.<engine_id>.<path>}` — a property from an upstream engine's
317	   *       yields (resolved at run time, dot-path traversal)
318	   *
319	   * When a string is exactly one expression (e.g. `${writ}`), the resolved
320	   * value preserves its original type. When expressions are embedded in a
321	   * larger string, the result is always a string.
322	   *
323	   * Non-string values are passed through literally.
324	   * Whole-value expressions that resolve to undefined cause the key to be omitted.
325	   * Inline expressions that resolve to undefined are replaced with empty string.
326	   *
327	   * Use `\${` to produce a literal `${` in the output without interpolation.
328	   */
329	  givens?: Record<string, unknown>;
330	  /**
331	   * Conditional activation expression. A `${yields.<engine_id>.<property>}` reference
332	   * (with optional `!` negation prefix) evaluated at runtime when the engine's upstream
333	   * is all done. When the condition is falsy, the engine is set to `skipped` status.
334	   * When absent, the engine is unconditional (always runs).
335	   *
336	   * Examples:
337	   *   '${yields.review.passed}'  — run this engine when review.passed is truthy
338	   *   '!${yields.review.passed}' — run this engine when review.passed is falsy
339	   */
340	  when?: string;
341	}
342	
343	/**
344	 * A complete rig template.
345	 */
346	export interface RigTemplate {
347	  /** Ordered list of engine slot declarations. */
348	  engines: RigTemplateEngine[];

840	/**
841	 * Spider-extended engine run result. Adds an optional `graft` field
842	 * to the `completed` variant, allowing engines to dynamically append
843	 * new engines to the rig alongside their yields.
844	 *
845	 * Engines that want to graft import this type from @shardworks/spider-apparatus.
846	 * Engines that don't graft use the base EngineRunResult from @shardworks/fabricator-apparatus.
847	 *
848	 * The Spider internally checks for the `graft` property on any completed result
849	 * (duck-typing — the Fabricator type is not modified).
850	 */
851	export type SpiderEngineRunResult =
852	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown; graft?: RigTemplateEngine[]; graftTail?: string }
853	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
854	  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
855	
856	/**
857	 * Spider-extended collect result. When a quick engine's collect() method
858	 * returns an object with a `graft` property (an array), the Spider extracts
859	 * it as a graft request and uses the `yields` property as the engine's yields.
860	 *
861	 * When collect() returns a value without a `graft` array property, the entire
862	 * return value is treated as yields (backward compatible).
863	 */
864	export interface SpiderCollectResult {
865	  yields: unknown;
866	  graft?: RigTemplateEngine[];
867	  graftTail?: string;
868	}
869	
870	// ── status.spider sub-slot shape ────────────────────────────────────────

Status update — Option B done.

## What landed

**Apparatus skeleton is fully wired.** A posted trial would crawl the rig cleanly end-to-end without any engine doing real work yet. The architectural risk Option B targeted (will the dynamic-graft pattern actually map onto the trial flow?) is resolved — yes, cleanly, using the same pattern `spider.implement-loop` uses.

- `lab.orchestrate` is the head engine in the template; everything else is grafted at runtime from `ext.laboratory.config`.
- Stubbed engines for codex/guild fixtures, scenario, probes, archive — each yields a placeholder so the chain completes successfully.
- 21 unit tests cover the graft-builder logic: topo-sort, upstream wiring, cycle detection, teardown-id derivation, empty-fixtures and empty-probes paths, given pass-through.
- Sanctum overrides expanded to all 22 framework + plugin packages — typechecking now matches live source.

Two commits in the cluster:
- `b306ab98` — apparatus skeleton (writ type, plugin reshape)
- `d6387a24` — rig template + skeleton engines

## Things I noticed that you might want to weigh in on

**Sequential teardowns vs. parallel.** I made fixture-teardowns sequential (each one waits on the previous). Reasoning: if a teardown fails, the rig stops without shredding siblings concurrently — gives us a chance to inspect state. But it's slower. Worth questioning if/when teardown perf matters; doesn't right now. Easy to flip later.

**Givens shared between paired setup/teardown.** A fixture decl has one `givens` block that flows to both engines. Most paired engines genuinely need the same context (codex name, repo URL, etc.), but this couples them. If a teardown ever needs *different* givens than its setup, we'd need to split them. Not blocking; flagging.

**Teardown-id convention.** `lab.codex-setup` ↔ `lab.codex-teardown` by default; explicit `teardownEngineId` overrides. Convention is implicit magic (some people hate that), but it shrinks the manifest. Override exists for cases where convention doesn't fit. Good enough I think — you'd only feel the magic in confusing error messages, and the convention is stable.

## Two things I want to bring up before continuing

**1. Live-guild reload.** This work is sitting on disk in vibers' linked plugin path. The running guild is using whatever was cached when it started. To actually verify the wiring runtime works, vibers needs a restart — but I don't want to do that without you knowing, in case you have something running. Want me to wait for a natural moment, or is now fine?

**2. Archive-target design (`c-momaa5o9`).** Still the open question from the original handoff. I've kept the archive engine as a stub yielding placeholder data for now; before I implement it for real, the lab-books-vs-sanctum-vs-hybrid question needs an answer. Three options to lay out when you're ready:

- **Lab-host guild's books** — captured data lives natively in the lab guild's stacks (writs, links, sessions, plus whatever new books we'd add). Cohesive: the lab guild becomes self-describing. Cost: schema design for capture-shaped data, querying cross-trial means crossing book boundaries.
- **Sanctum mirror** — captured data lands as files under `experiments/X.../artifacts/`. Continuity with the existing infrastructure spec. Cost: filesystem-as-database problems, two homes for what's conceptually one body of data.
- **Hybrid** — pointer in the lab's books, contents in the sanctum tree. Both surfaces queryable. Cost: more moving parts, schema for the linkage.

Want to design that now, or save it for later?

## Suggested next move

If we're not doing archive design yet: the **manifest CLI (`c-moma9ty6`)** is the natural next piece. With it, you could author a YAML manifest, post it as a trial via `nsg lab trial post --manifest <file>`, and watch the rig crawl. End-to-end flow would be exercisable (with stub engines still doing the work). That's a nice milestone before any real engine implementation.

Or if you'd rather see real work happen on a load-bearing engine, **`lab.guild-setup` (`c-momaa03d`)** is the gnarliest one (chains `nsg init` + per-pin `nsg plugin install` + `nsg codex add` + deep-merge + file copy) — knocking it out next would surface any framework-integration friction early.

content.text:
Two real architectural questions, stopping to think before answering. Let me work through both.

## Question 1: Flat orchestrate vs staged phase orchestrators

**Current (flat):** one `lab.orchestrate` engine reads the whole config and emits the entire graft chain.

**Alternative (staged):** the template enumerates 5 engines — `setup-phase`, `scenario-phase`, `probes-phase`, `archive-phase`, `teardown-phase` — each one orchestrating its own sub-graft.

I went flat initially because spider.implement-loop has only one orchestrating engine, and one-engine-grafts-everything seemed like the cleanest mapping. But thinking about it more, there's a real argument for staged:

| | Flat | Staged |
|---|---|---|
| Template documents flow | ✗ — template is a one-engine no-op | ✓ — template enumerates phases as the user-facing shape |
| Per-phase logic cohesion | ✗ — one big function with phase modes | ✓ — each phase orchestrator implements its own pattern (DAG sort, parallel scatter, reverse-sequential chain) |
| Per-phase failure visibility | ✗ — `lab.orchestrate` failed (which phase?) | ✓ — `lab.probes-phase` failed → I know which phase died |
| Extension point for new phases | ✗ — fork orchestrate.ts | △ — fork the template, but the *pattern* is in place |
| Code complexity | Lower (one engine) | Modestly higher (5 small engines + a shared helper) |

The "template documents the flow" point is what tipped me. The rig template is *the* user-facing shape document for a trial. Right now, the template is essentially `[ orchestrate ]` — it tells you nothing. With staged it tells you "this is how a trial flows."

**Concretely on cost:** the buildGraft function I wrote is actually already 5 per-phase blocks separated by comments. Splitting it into 5 helpers + 5 thin engine wrappers is mechanical refactoring, not redesign. Tests partition cleanly per-phase. Maybe a 30-minute change.

**Concretely on data flow:** all grafted engines share a namespace, so cross-phase upstream refs (e.g., `scenario.upstream: ['fixture-codex-setup']`) work fine. Phase orchestrators don't need to be data-isolated — each derives the engine ids it needs from the same trial config using a naming convention. Phases are organizational, not isolation boundaries.

**My position:** switch to staged. Cost is low, payoff is real (template-as-flow-doc, per-phase failure visibility, extension pattern), and nothing's locked in by going there.

## Question 2: Mechanical config-to-engine linkage

There are two readings of your question, and I want to make sure I answer the right one:

**Reading A: "as we add new probe ENGINES, can the substrate accommodate them automatically?"**

Already yes. Each config decl carries its `engineId`. Adding a new probe (say `lab.probe-cpu-profile`) means writing the engine and referencing it in some manifest's `probes:` list. No substrate code change. The probe-phase orchestrator iterates `config.probes[]` and grafts whatever engine each decl names — engine-agnostic.

**Reading B: "as we add new KINDS of phases, can the substrate accommodate them?"**

Today: no. Phases are hardcoded. Adding a "warmup" phase (say "spin up sample data before scenario") means forking the orchestrate engine, or with staged phases, forking the template. The substrate doesn't have a registry for "this is the set of phases; here's the order."

**Reading C: "should engines self-declare their phase role so the substrate can route mechanically?"**

This is a richer model — engines tag themselves with metadata like `phaseRole: 'probe'`, and the substrate queries "give me all probe-tagged engines." Manifest authors would just say "run probes" and get all of them.

I think this is overengineering for v1, and I want to push back gently. Reasons:

- **Manifests don't usually want all probes.** A trial that's measuring git-range output doesn't need a stacks-dump probe running. The selectivity is real, which means the manifest still needs explicit lists.
- **Engine metadata for phase role would be a framework change.** `EngineDesign` doesn't have a metadata bag; we'd be retrofitting one for the lab's use.
- **The categorical config shape is already serving as phase-routing metadata.** When you put an engine ref in `config.probes[]`, you're telling the substrate "this is a probe." That's mechanically linked. It's just that the linkage happens at the manifest authoring layer, not the engine declaration layer.

**Where I think a richer model might pay off later:** if/when an end user wants to plug in a new phase (Reading B), the cleanest extension is plugin contributions of the form "I provide a `validation` phase orchestrator + its config schema; please slot it after `archive`." That's a real extension model and worth designing once we have a real motivating use case. Not now.

## What I'd do

1. Refactor to staged orchestrators (5 phase engines, 5 helpers, template enumerates the phases).
2. Keep the categorical config shape (fixtures/scenario/probes/archive). Defer the engine-metadata-and-phase-registry richer model until someone wants a new phase.
3. Cross-phase data flow stays via direct upstream refs at the engine level (the engine id naming convention is the cross-phase contract).

Want me to do the refactor now, or do you want to push back on anything first?

After Sean's question about whether the rig template should set the overall structure (vs. one god engine grafting everything), worked through the trade-offs — staged wins on template-as-flow-doc, per-phase failure visibility (oculus shows lab.probes-phase failed instead of opaque orchestrator failure), per-phase logic cohesion (DAG sort vs parallel scatter vs reverse-sequential chain naturally split), and sets up an extension point for future phases (e.g., warmup before scenario). Cost was modest: existing buildGraft was already 5 phase blocks separated by comments. Split into 5 per-phase helpers (buildSetupGraft, buildScenarioGraft, buildProbesGraft, buildArchiveGraft, buildTeardownGraft) plus 5 phase orchestrator engines (lab.setup-phase, lab.scenario-phase, lab.probes-phase, lab.archive-phase, lab.teardown-phase). Template grew from 1 engine to 5 enumerating the flow. graftTail set only on lab.teardown-phase (the rig's true end for completion gating); intermediate phases let the next phase fire immediately, since real work-engines wait via explicit cross-phase upstream refs (scenario.upstream → all fixture-setups; probes.upstream → scenario; archive.upstream → all probes; teardowns chain back from archive). Cross-phase data flow stays at the work-engine layer — phase orchestrators don't relay yields. Sean confirmed: keep categorical config shape; reading-A (new probe engines accommodated automatically via engineId-on-decl) is what mechanical linkage already gives us; reading-B/C (new phases via plugin contribution + engine self-declared phase metadata) is later work. 29 tests now (up from 21), 7 suites — covers each per-phase graft helper plus an end-to-end composition test that asserts the staged phases yield the same flat work-engine list the prior flat orchestrator did. Build clean, typecheck clean."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum
EOF

input.command:
GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Coco GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=coco@nexus.local git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
laboratory: stage rig template into phase orchestrators

Refactor from a single `lab.orchestrate` engine that grafted the
whole trial chain into five phase orchestrators wired in sequence
through the rig template:

    setup-phase → scenario-phase → probes-phase → archive-phase
                                                → teardown-phase

Each phase orchestrator is a clockwork engine: it reads
`writ.ext.laboratory.config`, builds its phase's graft via a pure
helper (buildSetupGraft / buildScenarioGraft / buildProbesGraft /
buildArchiveGraft / buildTeardownGraft), and returns. Real work
engines are still grafted in at runtime; the template just
documents the phase backbone.

Why staged:
  - The template now documents the trial flow as the user-facing
    shape (was a one-engine no-op).
  - Per-phase failure visibility — oculus and rig introspection
    show "lab.probes-phase failed" instead of an opaque
    orchestrator-failed-somewhere.
  - Per-phase logic is cohesive (DAG sort, parallel scatter,
    reverse-sequential chain) — no big function with phase-mode
    flags.
  - Sets up the extension point for future phases (a plugin
    contributing `lab.warmup-phase` slots into a custom template
    alongside existing ones).

graftTail is set only on `lab.teardown-phase` (the rig's true end
for completion gating). Intermediate phases let the next phase
fire immediately upon return — real work engines wait via
explicit cross-phase upstream refs (scenario.upstream → every
fixture-setup; probes.upstream → scenario; archive.upstream →
every probe; teardowns chain back from archive).

Cross-phase data flow happens at the work-engine layer, not the
orchestrator layer. All grafted engines share a single namespace,
so direct upstream refs across phase boundaries work — phase
orchestrators don't relay yields between phases.

29 tests (up from 21), 7 suites — covers each per-phase graft
helper plus an end-to-end composition test asserting the staged
phases yield the same flat work-engine list the prior flat
orchestrator produced.

Refines the c-moma9vrm conclusion (already concluded): the
post-and-collect-default template now enumerates phases instead
of being a one-engine head.

Session: 88f89549-681a-4adf-9989-d1d76a2fdd85
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10

lastPrompt: Agree, refacto to staged. Keep the categorical config shape and overall rig phase structure. My question about automatically linking config to engines was just making sure we could handle reading "A".…

content.text:
Refactor done.

## Where we are

Three commits in the laboratory cluster:
- `b306ab98` — apparatus skeleton
- `d6387a24` — rig template + skeleton engines (flat orchestrate)
- `776db61f` — staged phase orchestrators (this commit)

The post-and-collect-default template now enumerates the phase backbone:

```
setup-phase → scenario-phase → probes-phase → archive-phase → teardown-phase
```

Each phase orchestrator reads the trial config and grafts its sub-rig. 29 tests across 7 suites validate the per-phase graft logic plus an end-to-end composition test that asserts the staged phases produce the same flat work-engine list the prior flat orchestrator did.

## Remaining lab implementation children

Concluded so far: framework verification, plugin reshape, trial writ type/schema, rig template (skeleton + staged refactor).

Still ahead:
- `c-moma9ty6` — manifest CLI
- `c-moma9y1k` — codex fixture engines (real)
- `c-momaa03d` — guild fixture engines (real)
- `c-momaa1vt` — cross-guild scenario engines (real)
- `c-momaa3w7` — probe engines (real)
- `c-momaa5o9` — archive design + impl (still has the open lab-vs-sanctum-vs-hybrid question)
- `c-momaa75l` — smoke test
- `c-momaa8mk` — retire infra spec + conclude c-molveg89
- `c-momaaa3t` — documentation
- `c-momaab8y` — port first real trial

## What's a good next move

Same options as before, with my preferences slightly updated:

**A. Manifest CLI (`c-moma9ty6`).** Unlocks end-to-end skeleton testing — author a YAML manifest, post a trial, watch the rig crawl through stub engines. Best path for empirically validating the wiring before implementing real engines. **My pick.**

**B. Real `lab.guild-setup` engine (`c-momaa03d`).** Most complex single engine; surfaces framework-integration friction early.

**C. Archive design conversation.** The open question's been sitting; resolving it unlocks the archive engine work and you've got context loaded.

I'd vote A. Writing the manifest CLI exercises the trial-config schema we declared in `types.ts` and the rig template we just staged, so we'd get fast feedback if anything in either is misshaped. After A lands, the apparatus is *operable* (manifest in → trial writ out → rig crawls), and we can prioritize the remaining work based on whatever feels most pressing.

Want me to head into the manifest CLI?

content: Building the Laboratory apparatus directly in the sanctum; just landed the staged phase-orchestrator refactor of the rig template (29 tests passing). Next up: the manifest CLI so we can post trials end-to-end, pending your go-ahead. (disable recaps in /config)

1	import { z } from 'zod';
2	import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
3	import { tool } from '@shardworks/tools-apparatus';
4	import type { ClerkApi } from '../types.ts';
5	
6	/**
7	 * Minimal structural subset of the codexes apparatus's `ScriptoriumApi`
8	 * that the commission-post handler relies on. Declared locally to keep
9	 * the codexes apparatus a soft (`recommends`) dependency — clerk does
10	 * not pull `@shardworks/codexes-apparatus` into its dependency graph.
11	 */
12	interface CodexRegistry {
13	  list(): Promise<Array<{ name: string }>>;
14	}
15	
16	export default tool({
17	  name: 'commission-post',
18	  description: 'Post a new commission, creating a writ in open or new (draft) phase',
19	  instructions:
20	    'Creates a new writ. By default the writ is placed in open phase and enters the queue ' +
21	    'immediately. Pass draft: true to create the writ in new (draft) phase instead — draft ' +
22	    'writs are held out of the queue until explicitly published with writ-publish. ' +
23	    'The writ type must be a type declared in the guild config, or the built-in type "mandate". ' +
24	    'If type is omitted, the guild\'s configured default type is used (defaults to "mandate"). ' +
25	    'Use parentId to create this writ as a child of an existing writ. The parent must be in ' +
26	    'new or open phase. ' +
27	    'When codex is omitted: if parentId is provided, the codex is inherited from the parent ' +
28	    'writ; otherwise, when the guild has exactly one registered codex it is selected ' +
29	    'automatically, when the guild has two or more registered codexes the call fails with ' +
30	    'an error naming the candidates, and when no codexes are registered the call fails ' +
31	    'asking the operator to install or declare one.',
32	  params: {
33	    title: z.string().describe('Short human-readable title describing the work'),
34	    body: z.string().describe('Detail text or description'),
35	    type: z.string().optional().describe('Writ type (default: guild defaultType or "mandate")'),
36	    codex: z
37	      .string()
38	      .optional()
39	      .describe(
40	        'Target codex name. Optional. When omitted: inherited from parentId when provided; ' +
41	        'otherwise defaulted to the single registered codex when the guild has exactly one; ' +
42	        'rejected at post time when the guild has two or more (error names the candidates) or ' +
43	        'when no codexes are registered.',
44	      ),
45	    draft: z
46	      .boolean()
47	      .optional()
48	      .describe(
49	        'When true, create the writ in new (draft) phase instead of open. ' +
50	        'Draft writs must be published before they enter the execution queue.',
51	      ),
52	    parentId: z
53	      .string()
54	      .optional()
55	      .describe(
56	        'Create this writ as a child of the specified parent writ. ' +
57	        'The parent must be in new or open phase.',
58	      ),
59	  },
60	  permission: 'write',
61	  handler: async (params) => {
62	    const clerk = guild().apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk');
63	    const resolvedParentId = params.parentId
64	      ? await clerk.resolveId(params.parentId)
65	      : undefined;
66	
67	    // Resolve codex up-front when the caller did not supply one and is
68	    // not relying on parent inheritance. Two cases collapse to a hard
69	    // error here so that no codex-less writ enters the queue: a guild
70	    // with multiple registered codexes (ambiguous default) and a guild
71	    // with none (nothing to inherit from). A guild with exactly one
72	    // registered codex defaults silently. When parentId is supplied we
73	    // skip resolution entirely and let clerk.post()'s existing parent-
74	    // inheritance branch fire.
75	    let resolvedCodex = params.codex;
76	    if (resolvedCodex === undefined && resolvedParentId === undefined) {
77	      const codexes = guild().tryApparatus<CodexRegistry>('codexes');
78	      const registered = codexes ? await codexes.list() : [];
79	      const names = registered
80	        .map((entry) => entry.name)
81	        .sort((a, b) => a.localeCompare(b));
82	      if (names.length === 1) {
83	        resolvedCodex = names[0];
84	      } else if (names.length === 0) {
85	        throw new Error(
86	          'no codexes are registered; install a codex package or declare one in guild.json before posting commissions',
87	        );
88	      } else {
89	        throw new Error(
90	          `commission-post: --codex is required when the guild has multiple codexes (registered: ${names.join(', ')})`,
91	        );
92	      }
93	    }
94	
95	    const writ = await clerk.post({
96	      title: params.title,
97	      body: params.body,
98	      type: params.type,
99	      codex: resolvedCodex,
100	      parentId: resolvedParentId,
101	    });
102	
103	    // Auto-publish for mandate writs: `post()` always lands the writ in its
104	    // type's declared initial state, which for mandate is `new` (draft).
105	    // The commission-post tool preserves its prior UX — by default the
106	    // posted writ enters the queue immediately — by transitioning the
107	    // writ to `open` here when the caller did not request a draft. The
108	    // auto-advance is deliberately confined to mandate: for other
109	    // plugin-registered types the initial state is the caller's only
110	    // landing spot, and advancing without a type-specific tool would be
111	    // silent coupling.
112	    if (params.draft !== true && writ.type === 'mandate') {
113	      return clerk.transition(writ.id, 'open');
114	    }
115	    return writ;
116	  },
117	});
118

input.description: Find ext field handling in clerk types

content.content:
130	   * The semantic distinction matters: `status` is for what a plugin
131	   * has *observed* about a writ after the fact (a stuck cause, a
132	   * triggering child, a gate result); `ext` is for what a plugin needs
133	   * the writ to *carry* as an attribute of its identity (a petition id,
134	   * a classifier tag, a foreign-system reference). Picking the wrong
135	   * slot layers metadata under an observation contract or vice versa,
136	   * so plugin authors should choose consciously.
137	   *
138	   * Structurally identical to `status`: a plugin-keyed map
139	   * (`Record<PluginId, unknown>`) where each top-level key is a plugin
140	   * id and the value is an arbitrary opaque shape. Ownership is
141	   * convention-only — plugin `X` writes only to `ext[X]`.
142	   *
143	   * There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path:
144	   * `ClerkApi.setWritExt(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a
145	   * transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId`
146	   * so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
147	   * `transition()` silently strips `ext` from its body, and the
148	   * generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not
149	   * supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than
150	   * `setWritExt()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber
151	   * sibling sub-slots. Readers access `writ.ext?.[pluginId]` directly.
152	   *
153	   * Optional and absent by default — a freshly-posted writ reads
154	   * `ext === undefined` until a plugin writes its first sub-slot. The
155	   * slot survives terminal phase transitions.
156	   */
157	  ext?: Record<string, unknown>;
158	  /** Short human-readable title. */
159	  title: string;
160	  /** Detail text. */
161	  body: string;
162	  /** Target codex name. */
163	  codex?: string;
164	  /** Parent writ id. Absent on root writs. Immutable after creation. */
165	  parentId?: string;
166	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ was created. */
167	  createdAt: string;
168	  /** ISO timestamp of the last mutation. */
169	  updatedAt: string;
170	  /** ISO timestamp when the writ reached a terminal state. */
171	  resolvedAt?: string;
172	  /** Summary of how the writ resolved (set on any terminal transition). */
173	  resolution?: string;
174	}
175	
176	// ── Requests ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
177	
178	/**
179	 * Request to post a new commission (create a writ).
180	 */
181	/**
182	 * Request to edit a writ. Title and body can be edited in any status.
183	 * Type and codex can only be changed while the writ is in 'new' status.
184	 * All fields are optional — only provided fields are updated.
185	 */
186	export interface EditWritRequest {
187	  /** Writ id. */
188	  id: string;
189	  /** New title. */
190	  title?: string;
191	  /** New body text. */
192	  body?: string;
193	  /** New writ type. Must be a valid declared type. */
194	  type?: string;
195	  /** New target codex name. Pass empty string to clear. */
196	  codex?: string;
197	}
198	
199	export interface PostCommissionRequest {
200	  /**
201	   * Writ type. Defaults to the guild's configured defaultType, or "mandate"
202	   * if no default is configured. Must be a registered writ type.
203	   */
204	  type?: string;

content.text:
The tool is plumbed end-to-end — `nsg lab-trial-post` shows up in help with the manifest as a positional arg and `--draft` as a flag. I'm not going to actually post a real trial against vibers without you confirming the daemon-restart timing (post-without-restart would leave a half-stuck trial writ if the daemon's spider hasn't reloaded).

One thing to flag: the CLI form is `nsg lab-trial-post`, not `nsg lab trial-post`. The framework's auto-grouping needs ≥2 hyphenated tools sharing a prefix to activate. With only one `lab-`-prefixed tool, the prefix doesn't get promoted to a subcommand. As soon as a second lab tool lands (likely `lab-trial-show` or `lab-trial-cancel` once we want them), it'll auto-group into `nsg lab <subcommand>`.

Now committing.

Codex auto-resolution mirrors commission-post (inherit from parentId; default to single registered codex; fail when multiple/none). 26 manifest tests added (55 total): YAML parse errors, schema rejections (slug shape, missing required, malformed ids, mutability enum), defaults (empty fixtures/probes, empty givens), cross-field DAG validation passthrough, probe id uniqueness, manifestToWritShape (title default 'Trial: <slug>', description→body, parentId/codex pass-through, trialConfig packing). laboratory: manifest CLI — `nsg lab-trial-post`

as a trial writ in one step. The manifest mirrors
LaboratoryTrialConfig's shape with optional title, description,
parentId, and codex fields (description lands on the writ body;
title defaults to "Trial: <slug>").

uniqueness → manifestToWritShape pure-data split → clerk.post

parentId when provided; default to the single registered codex
when the guild has exactly one; fail-loud with the candidate list
when there are multiple, and with an install hint when there are
none.

alphanumeric and hyphens, ≤40 chars — keep `trial-<slug>-<trialId-
prefix>` codex/GH-repo names under the 63-char DNS limit with
